chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov (1927): "Any external stimulus which is to become the signal in a conditioned reflex must overlap in point of time with the action of an unconditioned stimulus." In delay conditioning, the CS onset precedes US onset with temporal overlap, and this arrangement is the most effective for establishing classical conditioning.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Herrnstein, R.J. (1961). Relative and absolute strength of response as a function of frequency of reinforcement. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 4(3), 267-272: "The relative frequency of responding on a key equaled the relative frequency of reinforcement from it." The matching law states that the relative rate of responding on a particular alternative matches the relative rate of reinforcement obtained from that alternative. When two or more concurrent schedules operate simultaneously, the organism distributes its behavior proportionally to the reinforcement each schedule delivers.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Rescorla, R.A. (1988). Pavlovian conditioning: It's not what you think it is. American Psychologist, 43(3), 151-160: "The previously neutral conditioned stimulus comes to elicit the response" after pairing with the unconditioned stimulus. "One, initially neutral in that it elicits no response, is called the conditioned stimulus (CS); the other, which is one that consistently elicits a response, is called the unconditioned stimulus (US)."

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov (1927): "If a tone of 1000 d.v. is established as a conditioned stimulus, many other tones spontaneously acquire similar properties, such properties diminishing proportionally to the intervals of these tones from the one of 1000 d.v." Stimuli resembling the original CS elicit the CR despite never being directly paired with the US.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov (1927): "The hitherto quiet dog began to squeal in its stand, kept wriggling about, tore off with its teeth the apparatus for mechanical stimulation of the skin." "The clashing of excitation with inhibition led to a profound disturbance of the usual balance between these two processes." Experimental neurosis arises when an organism must discriminate between highly similar stimuli.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Watson, J.B. & Rayner, R. (1920). Conditioned emotional reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 3(1), 1-14: After conditioning Little Albert to fear the white rat, "the response to the rabbit, the dog, and the fur coat were [also] evoked." Albert's fear response to other white, furry stimuli -- though never directly paired with the loud noise -- demonstrates stimulus generalization: stimuli resembling the original CS elicit the CR without separate conditioning.

chapter_title: Beyond the Basics: Higher-Order Conditioning, Blocking, and Overshadowing
verbatim: Kamin, L.J. (1969). Predictability, surprise, attention, and conditioning. In B.A. Campbell & R.M. Church (Eds.), Punishment and aversive behavior (pp. 279-296): Prior conditioning to stimulus A "blocks" conditioning to stimulus B when both are presented as a compound AB paired with the US. The US must be "surprising" for new learning to occur. When the US is already fully predicted by the pre-trained CS, no additional associative strength accrues to the new stimulus presented simultaneously.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Terrace, H.S. (1963). Discrimination learning with and without "errors." Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 6(1), 1-27: "S--'s duration and brightness is progressively increased until S+ and S-- differ only with respect to wavelength." Fading is the systematic and gradual removal of prompts (supplementary cues such as brightness or size differences) so that behavior comes under the control of the natural discriminative stimulus alone.

chapter_title: Beyond the Basics: Higher-Order Conditioning, Blocking, and Overshadowing
verbatim: Mackintosh, N.J. (1976). Overshadowing and stimulus intensity. Animal Learning & Behavior, 4(2), 186-192: "Pavlov (1927) used the term 'overshadowing' to describe the observation that conditioning to a relatively weak stimulus might be severely attenuated if it was always presented in conjunction with a more intense stimulus." "When one component was markedly more salient than the other, the more salient overshadowed the less salient."

chapter_title: Beyond the Basics: Higher-Order Conditioning, Blocking, and Overshadowing
verbatim: Rizley, R.C. & Rescorla, R.A. (1972). Associations in second-order conditioning and sensory preconditioning. Journal of Comparative and Physiological Psychology, 81(1), 1-11: "A typical second-order conditioning situation in which S1 is first paired with a US and then S2 is regularly followed by S1. As a consequence of the first stage, presentation of S1 elicits a CR, while during the second-order conditioning stage S2 also comes to elicit that CR." CS2 elicits a CR even though it was never directly paired with the original US.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov, I.P. (1927). Conditioned reflexes: An investigation of the physiological activity of the cerebral cortex. Oxford University Press: "Any external stimulus which is to become the signal in a conditioned reflex must overlap in point of time with the action of an unconditioned stimulus." In delay conditioning, the CS onset precedes and temporally overlaps the US onset, and this arrangement is the most effective method for establishing a conditioned response.

chapter_title: Beyond the Basics: Higher-Order Conditioning, Blocking, and Overshadowing
verbatim: Kamin (1969): Prior conditioning to stimulus A prevents conditioning to stimulus B when both are presented as a compound AB paired with the US. The US must be surprising for learning to occur. If the US is fully predicted by Stimulus A, no new learning about Stimulus B occurs.

chapter_title: Beyond the Basics: Higher-Order Conditioning, Blocking, and Overshadowing
verbatim: Pavlov (1927): Higher-order (second-order) conditioning involves first establishing a conditioned stimulus (CS1) by pairing it with an unconditioned stimulus, then pairing a new neutral stimulus (CS2) with the already-established CS1. "A well-established conditioned stimulus is used in the place of the unconditioned stimulus" so that CS2 also acquires the ability to elicit a conditioned response when presented alone, without ever having been directly paired with the unconditioned stimulus.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov (1927): "Repeated application of a conditioned stimulus which is not followed up by reinforcement leads to a weakening of the conditioned reflex." "Left to themselves extinguished conditioned reflexes spontaneously recover their full strength after a longer or shorter interval of time." "It cannot be regarded as an irreparable destruction of the conditioned reflex... evidenced by the fact that the extinguished reflexes spontaneously regenerate in course of time." Extinction suppresses rather than eliminates learned associations.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Rescorla, R.A. (1988). Pavlovian conditioning: It's not what you think it is. American Psychologist, 43(3), 151-160: The CR is typically similar to but may differ from the UR, and is generally less intense. Rescorla notes "an important, but poorly appreciated, fact about conditioning that makes nonsense of any claim that a signal simply acquires the ability to evoke the response to the US."

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Thorndike (1911): Thorndike's research with cats in a puzzle box led to his development of the law of effect: "Of several responses made to the same situation, those which are accompanied or closely followed by satisfaction to the animal will, other things being equal, be more firmly connected with the situation... those which are accompanied or closely followed by discomfort to the animal will, other things being equal, have their connections with that situation weakened."

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Dinsmoor, J.A. (1977). Escape, avoidance, punishment. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 28(1), 83-95: "More rapid termination of the shock selectively reinforced the behavior of holding the bar down." Escape conditioning is a form of negative reinforcement in which a behavior terminates an ongoing aversive stimulus.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Skinner (1953): "We define the negative reinforcer (an aversive stimulus) as any stimulus the withdrawal of which strengthens the behavior." Behavior that terminates an aversive stimulus is strengthened by removal of that stimulus.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Moher, C.A. et al. (2008). Non-generalized and generalized conditioned reinforcers. Behavioral Interventions, 23(1), 13-38: Generalized conditioned reinforcers "may be less sensitive to the effects of satiation" because their effectiveness "does not depend on a single kind of deprivation." "Increasing the number of backup reinforcers with which the token was paired resulted in the effectiveness maintaining during satiation conditions."

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Skinner, B.F. (1953). Science and human behavior. Macmillan: A positive discriminative stimulus (S^D) "sets the occasion" for a response by signaling that reinforcement is available. In its presence, the operant response is reinforced; in its absence, the response goes unreinforced. The S^D does not elicit the behavior but rather indicates that reinforcement is contingent upon the response.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Lerman, D.C. & Vorndran, C.M. (2002). On the status of knowledge for using punishment. Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis, 35(4), 431-464: Negative punishment involves "the removal of stimuli, often called negative punishment (i.e., response cost, time-out from positive reinforcement)." The removal of a desirable stimulus following a behavior decreases the likelihood of that behavior occurring in the future.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Mowrer, O.H. (1956). Two-factor learning theory reconsidered. Psychological Review, 63(2), 114-128: "Basically the two-factor, or two-process, hypothesis holds that habits are learned on the basis of the reinforcement provided by reward, or drive reduction, and that fears are learned (conditioned) on the basis of the contiguous occurrence of a signal and punishment." Avoidance involves (1) classical conditioning of fear to a neutral stimulus and (2) operant conditioning of avoidance behavior reinforced by fear reduction.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Mowrer, O.H. (1956). Two-factor learning theory reconsidered. Psychological Review, 63(2), 114-128: "Basically the two-factor, or two-process, hypothesis holds that habits are learned on the basis of the reinforcement provided by reward, or drive reduction, and that fears are learned (conditioned) on the basis of the contiguous occurrence of a signal and punishment." Avoidance conditioning combines (1) classical conditioning of fear to a neutral stimulus and (2) operant conditioning (negative reinforcement) in which the avoidance behavior is maintained because it reduces conditioned fear.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Reynolds, G.S. (1961). Behavioral contrast. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 4(1), 57-71: "The increase in the rate of responding during one component of a multiple schedule when the conditions in another component are changed so as to decrease the rate of responding during that component" constitutes behavioral contrast. When reinforcement is discontinued for one behavior but maintained for another, the still-reinforced behavior increases in rate -- a phenomenon termed behavioral contrast.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Reynolds, G.S. (1961). Behavioral contrast. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 4(1), 57-71: Positive behavioral contrast occurs when reinforcement for one behavior in a multiple schedule is discontinued while reinforcement for the other behavior remains unchanged, resulting in the still-reinforced behavior increasing in rate above its original baseline level, while the no-longer-reinforced behavior decreases.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Hagopian, L.P. et al. (2011). Reinforcement schedule thinning following functional communication training. Behavior Analysis in Practice, 4(1), 4-16: Schedule thinning involves "altering the reinforcement schedule, usually in a systematic and progressive manner across multiple sessions, until some terminal schedule, judged to be practical for care providers to implement, is reached." The systematic, gradual transition from dense to lean reinforcement schedules.

chapter_title: Applied Principles and the Bridge to Research Methods
verbatim: Reynolds, G.S. (1961). Behavioral contrast. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 4(1), 57-71: When reinforcement is stopped for one of two concurrently reinforced responses (e.g., pressing Bar B), positive behavioral contrast predicts that the still-reinforced response (pressing Bar A) will increase in frequency while the no-longer-reinforced response (pressing Bar B) decreases.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Dinsmoor, J.A. (1977). Escape, avoidance, punishment. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 28(1), 83-95: "The only requirement is that these responses produce earlier termination of the shock when the animal is holding the bar down at the moment of onset than when it is somewhere else, off the bar." The behavior that results in removal of the aversive stimulus is the one being negatively reinforced. Always identify whose behavior is being strengthened by the removal of the aversive stimulus -- that individual is under negative reinforcement control.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Hagopian, L.P. et al. (2011). Reinforcement schedule thinning following functional communication training. Behavior Analysis in Practice, 4(1), 4-16: Schedule thinning involves "altering the reinforcement schedule, usually in a systematic and progressive manner across multiple sessions, until some terminal schedule, judged to be practical for care providers to implement, is reached." Thinning is the gradual transition from a denser (thicker) to a leaner (thinner) reinforcement schedule.

chapter_title: Applied Principles and the Bridge to Research Methods
verbatim: Skinner, B.F. (1948). "Superstition" in the pigeon. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 38(2), 168-172: Skinner referred to the unusual behaviors exhibited by pigeons -- such as bowing, turning, and hopping on one foot -- as "superstitious behavior." When pigeons were delivered food pellets every 15 seconds regardless of their behavior, they developed ritualistic responses as if those behaviors caused the food delivery. "The bird behaves as if there were a causal relation between its behavior and the presentation of food, although such a relation is lacking."

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Lerman, D.C. et al. (1996). Responding maintained by intermittent reinforcement. Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis, 29(2), 153-171: "Switching from an INT to a CRF schedule prior to extinction may lower the baseline response rate." Continuous reinforcement carries a high risk of satiation, while transitioning to intermittent reinforcement reduces satiation risk and maintains behavior.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Terrace, H.S. (1963). Discrimination learning with and without "errors." Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 6(1), 1-27: "S--'s duration and brightness is progressively increased until S+ and S-- differ only with respect to wavelength." By gradually changing stimuli, organisms can learn discriminations "without errors." Fading involves the gradual withdrawal of auxiliary stimulus differences (prompts) so that behavior comes under control of the natural discriminative stimulus alone.

chapter_title: Consequences That Shape Behavior: Reinforcement, Punishment, and the Operant Framework
verbatim: Baer, D.M., Wolf, M.M., & Risley, T.R. (1968). Some current dimensions of applied behavior analysis. Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis, 1(1), 91-97: The discriminative stimulus (SD) signals that reinforcement is available for a particular response; in its presence a response is reinforced, and in its absence it goes unreinforced.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Nevin, J.A. & Grace, R.C. (2000). Behavioral momentum and the Law of Effect. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 23(1), 73-90: "Variable-ratio (VR) schedules usually maintain much higher response rates than VI schedules." The variable ratio schedule produces the highest and most consistent response rate among the four basic intermittent schedules. When using positive reinforcement to establish or increase a behavior, use of the variable ratio schedule maximizes resistance to extinction.

chapter_title: Schedules, Shaping, and the Fine-Tuning of Behavior
verbatim: Nevin, J.A. & Grace, R.C. (2000). Behavioral momentum and the Law of Effect. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 23(1), 73-90: "Variable-ratio (VR) schedules usually maintain much higher response rates than VI schedules." Among the four basic intermittent schedules (FR, VR, FI, VI), the variable-ratio schedule produces the highest and most consistent response rates and the greatest resistance to extinction.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Watson, J.B. & Rayner, R. (1920). Conditioned emotional reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 3(1), 1-14: "The instant the rat was shown, the bar was struck immediately behind his head." The CS (white rat) was presented and overlapped in time with the US (loud noise), consistent with delay conditioning -- the CS precedes and temporally overlaps the US. Watson used this forward/delay conditioning procedure to establish Little Albert's conditioned fear response to the white rat.

chapter_title: The Architecture of Association: How Classical Conditioning Builds Learned Responses
verbatim: Pavlov, I.P. (1927). Conditioned reflexes: An investigation of the physiological activity of the cerebral cortex. Oxford University Press: "Left to themselves extinguished conditioned reflexes spontaneously recover their full strength after a longer or shorter interval of time." "It cannot be regarded as an irreparable destruction of the conditioned reflex." Spontaneous recovery after extinction trials demonstrates that extinction is due to internal inhibition (active suppression) rather than the erasure of the learned association.

Gueorguieva, R. & Krystal, J.H. (2004). Move over ANOVA. Archives of General Psychiatry, 61(3), 310-317: Designs involving "multiple measurements of the same individual" where subjects are "randomly assigned to treatment groups" (between-subjects factor) and measured at multiple time points (within-subjects factor). A mixed design combines at least one between-subjects factor and one within-subjects factor.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: "When samples are drawn from population N (mu, sigma-squared) with a sample size of n, the distribution of the sample mean should be a normal distribution N (mu, sigma-squared/n)." The standard error of the mean (SE = sigma / sqrt(n)) increases as the population standard deviation increases and as the sample size decreases.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Gueorguieva, R. & Krystal, J.H. (2004). Move over ANOVA. Archives of General Psychiatry, 61(3), 310-317: MANOVA analyzes group differences when there are multiple dependent variables measured at the interval/ratio level. The multivariate analysis of variance simultaneously tests the effects of one or more independent variables on two or more dependent variables, controlling the experimentwise Type I error rate.

Rubin, D.B. (1974). Estimating causal effects of treatments in randomized and nonrandomized studies. Journal of Educational Psychology, 66(5), 688-701: "If the treatments are randomly assigned, the difference in Y between experimental and control units is an 'unbiased' estimate of the desired typical causal effect." Random assignment gives each participant an equal probability of being in each group, equating the groups on all factors except the treatment.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Kornbrot, D. (2014). Point biserial correlation. In Wiley StatsRef: "The point biserial correlation, r_pb, is the value of Pearson's product moment correlation when one of the variables is dichotomous, taking on only two possible values coded 0 and 1, and the other variable is metric (interval or ratio)." The point biserial correlation coefficient is used when one variable is a true dichotomy (e.g., high school diploma: yes/no) and the other is continuous (e.g., yearly income in dollars).

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Slack, M.K. & Draugalis, J.R. (2001). Establishing the internal and external validity of experimental studies. American Journal of Health-System Pharmacy, 58(22), 2173-2181: "History becomes a threat when other factors external to the subjects (in addition to the treatment variable) occur by virtue of the passage of time." In a study examining the effects of an anti-drug program on middle school students' attitudes toward drug use, history would be the biggest threat to internal validity, as external events during the study period (e.g., media campaigns, community events) may systematically affect the students' attitudes independent of the program.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Mason, C.H. & Perreault, W.D. (1991). Collinearity, power, and interpretation of multiple regression analysis. Journal of Marketing Research, 28(3), 268-280: "Collinearity is generally agreed to be present if there is an approximate linear relationship (i.e., shared variance) among some of the predictor variables" and "near collinearities inflate the variances of the regression coefficients."

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: For the t test of independent means, "d = (m_A - m_B) / sigma" with conventional benchmarks of Small = .20, Medium = .50, Large = .80. A Cohen's d of .60 falls in the medium range and is interpreted in terms of standard deviation units -- meaning the two group means differ by .60 standard deviations.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: "The risk of mistakenly rejecting the null hypothesis (H_0) and thus of committing a Type I error, alpha, represents a policy: the maximum risk of a false rejection." Alpha and beta are inversely related: changing alpha from .01 to .05 increases the probability of a Type I error (false rejection of a true null hypothesis) and decreases the probability of a Type II error (failure to reject a false null hypothesis), thereby increasing statistical power.

Israel et al. (1998): CBPR is "a partnership approach to research that equitably involves community members, organizational representatives, and academic researchers in all aspects of the research process."

chapter_title: Applied Principles and the Bridge to Research Methods
verbatim: Denzin (1970/2017): Triangulation is "the combination of two or more theories, sources of data, research methods, in the study of a singular phenomenon." Between-methods triangulation specifically involves combining both qualitative and quantitative methods to collect data on the same research question, enhancing confidence in findings by leveraging the complementary strengths of different methodological approaches.

Stevens, S. S. (1946): Both interval and ratio scales permit arithmetic operations on differences because their intervals are equal by definition. "Determination of equality of intervals or differences."

Sawyer, S.F. (2009). Analysis of variance: The fundamental concepts. Journal of Manual & Manipulative Therapy, 17(2), 27E-38E: "When an ANOVA has two or more independent variables it is referred to as a factorial ANOVA." Independent variables are manipulated or categorized variables (e.g., SAT review program, sex, test anxiety level = 3 IVs); dependent variables are the measured outcomes (e.g., SAT verbal score, SAT quantitative score = 2 DVs). The study described has 3 independent variables and 2 dependent variables.

von Hippel, P.T. (2005). Mean, median, and skew: Correcting a textbook rule. Journal of Statistics Education, 13(2): "The mean is pulled in the direction of the extreme scores or tail (same as the direction of the skew), and the median is between the mean and the mode." In a positively skewed distribution, the positive tail pulls the mean to the highest value, the mode clusters at the peak (lowest value), and the median falls between them: mode < median < mean.

von Hippel, P.T. (2005). Mean, median, and skew: Correcting a textbook rule. Journal of Statistics Education, 13(2): "In a skewed distribution, the mean is farther out in the long tail than is the median" and "the order is always from mode, to median, to mean" moving from peak toward the long tail. In a positively skewed distribution, the mode is the lowest score (at the peak), and the mean is the highest score (pulled toward the positive tail).

von Hippel, P.T. (2005). Mean, median, and skew: Correcting a textbook rule. Journal of Statistics Education, 13(2): The mean is "pulled in the direction of the extreme scores or tail," making it unrepresentative in skewed distributions. The median is resistant to such distortion because it divides the distribution into equal halves regardless of extreme values. The median is preferred for skewed data because it is not influenced by the magnitude of extreme observations.

Palinkas, L.A. et al. (2015). Purposeful sampling for qualitative data collection and analysis in mixed method implementation research. Administration and Policy in Mental Health, 42(5), 533-544: "Probabilistic or random sampling is used to ensure the generalizability of findings by minimizing the potential for bias in selection." Cluster sampling is classified as a form of probability sampling in which the population is divided into naturally occurring groups (clusters), and entire clusters are randomly selected.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Barnett, A.G. et al. (2005). Regression to the mean: What it is and how to deal with it. International Journal of Epidemiology, 34(1), 215-220: "Relatively high (or relatively low) observations are likely to be followed by less extreme ones nearer the subject's true mean." Statistical regression threatens internal validity when participants are selected based on extreme scores; on retesting, those scores will naturally tend toward the population mean, which can be mistakenly attributed to the treatment.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Braver, M.W. & Braver, S.L. (1988). Statistical treatment of the Solomon four-group design. Psychological Bulletin, 104(1), 150-154: The design is "the only one of the three able to assess the presence of pretest sensitization" because it includes groups without pretesting. Controls for pretest sensitization by including pretested and non-pretested versions of both treatment and control groups.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Brooks, J.L. (2012). Counterbalancing for serial order carryover effects in experimental condition orders. Psychological Methods, 17(4), 600-614: "Each condition appears in each serial position equally often." A Latin square ensures each treatment appears in every ordinal position an equal number of times, controlling for order and sequence effects.

Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: The 68-95-99.7 rule (empirical rule): 68% of data fall within +/-1 SD of the mean, 95% within +/-2 SD, and 99.7% within +/-3 SD.

Baron & Kenny (1986): "A moderator is a qualitative (e.g., sex, race, class) or quantitative (e.g., level of reward) variable that affects the direction and/or strength of the relation between an independent or predictor variable and a dependent or criterion variable."

Baron, R.M. & Kenny, D.A. (1986). The moderator-mediator variable distinction in social psychological research. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 51(6), 1173-1182: "In general, a given variable may be said to function as a mediator to the extent that it accounts for the relation between the predictor and the criterion." "Mediators speak to how or why such effects occur." When parenting style mediates the relationship between marital conflict and child adjustment, it represents the mechanism through which marital conflict influences child outcomes.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Steckler, A. & McLeroy, K.R. (2008). The importance of external validity. American Journal of Public Health, 98(1), 9-10: External validity concerns "whether causal relationships can be generalized to different measures, persons, settings, and times." External validity refers to the degree to which research findings can be generalized beyond the specific conditions of the study.

Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs: Uses in applied clinical research. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: The multiple baseline design applies treatment sequentially across different behaviors, settings, or individuals while monitoring all baselines simultaneously. It avoids the ethical concern of withdrawing an effective treatment (as in the ABAB design) and is best suited when a client has multiple target behaviors (e.g., storm, height, and spider phobias) that can be treated sequentially.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Barnett, A.G. et al. (2005). Regression to the mean: What it is and how to deal with it. International Journal of Epidemiology, 34(1), 215-220: "Relatively high (or relatively low) observations are likely to be followed by less extreme ones nearer the subject's true mean." When participants are selected for a study because they obtained extreme scores (e.g., high test anxiety), statistical regression is the biggest threat to internal validity because their scores will naturally move toward the mean on retesting, regardless of treatment.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Slack, M.K. & Draugalis, J.R. (2001). Establishing the internal and external validity of experimental studies. American Journal of Health-System Pharmacy, 58(22), 2173-2181: "Internal validity is the degree to which a study establishes the cause-and-effect relationship between the treatment and the observed outcome." Findings can be attributed to the independent variable rather than confounds.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Braver, M.W. & Braver, S.L. (1988). Statistical treatment of the Solomon four-group design. Psychological Bulletin, 104(1), 150-154: The Solomon four-group design is "the only one of the three able to assess the presence of pretest sensitization" because it includes pretested and non-pretested versions of both treatment and control groups. It evaluates the effects of pretesting on both internal and external validity.

Wahlsten, D. (1991). Sample size to detect a planned contrast and a one degree-of-freedom interaction effect. Psychological Bulletin, 110(3), 587-595: "Interactions should be examined before Main Effects. If interactions are not statistically significant, then Main Effects can be easily evaluated." When a significant interaction is present, main effects cannot be interpreted independently.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: "The sampling distribution shows normal distribution with mean of mu and variance of sigma-squared/n." The standard error formula SE = sigma/sqrt(n) shows the standard error is directly proportional to the population standard deviation and inversely proportional to the square root of the sample size.

Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: The A-B-A-B design "has the added advantage of providing two occasions for the treatment variable to demonstrate a positive effect, thus further strengthening conclusions as to its actions." Repeated introduction and withdrawal of treatment strengthens causal inference.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Day, S.J. & Altman, D.G. (2000). Blinding in clinical trials and other studies. BMJ, 321(7259), 504: "In a double blind trial neither the patient nor the caregivers are aware of the treatment assignment." Controls for both participant expectancy effects and experimenter bias.

chapter_title: Threats from Within and Without: Research Design and Validity
verbatim: Rubin, D.B. (1974). Estimating causal effects of treatments in randomized and nonrandomized studies. Journal of Educational Psychology, 66(5), 688-701: "If the treatments are randomly assigned, the difference in Y between experimental and control units is an 'unbiased' estimate of the desired typical causal effect." Random assignment equalizes groups on both known and unknown confounds.

Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: "A further design... known as the A-B-A-B design... meets this criticism as study ends on the B or treatment phase. This design has the added advantage of providing two occasions for the treatment variable to demonstrate a positive effect." The ABAB design alternates baseline (A) and treatment (B) phases, using identical intervention in both treatment conditions.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: "The risk of mistakenly rejecting the null hypothesis (H_0) and thus of committing a Type I error, alpha, represents a policy: the maximum risk of a false rejection." Alpha and beta are inversely related: raising alpha increases Type I error risk but decreases beta (Type II error probability).

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: "Unlike group designs, however, continuous measurements are taken throughout each phase of the study." "Baseline measurements are generally continued until a stable pattern emerges." Single-subject designs require continuous measurement of the target behavior at regular intervals throughout all phases.

Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs: Uses in applied clinical research. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: "Baseline measurements are generally continued until a stable pattern emerges." The time-series design is a within-subjects (group) design that extends the logic of the single-subject AB design to a group of participants, measuring the dependent variable at multiple time points before and after an intervention.

Rubin, D.B. (1974). Estimating causal effects of treatments in randomized and nonrandomized studies. Journal of Educational Psychology, 66(5), 688-701: "If the treatments are randomly assigned, the difference in Y between experimental and control units is an 'unbiased' estimate of the desired typical causal effect." True experimental research is distinguished from quasi-experimental research by two features: (1) the researcher manipulates the independent variable, and (2) participants are randomly assigned to conditions. Quasi-experimental designs lack random assignment.

Barlow, D.H. & Hersen, M. (1973). Single-case experimental designs: Uses in applied clinical research. Archives of General Psychiatry, 29(3), 319-325: The ABAB design requires withdrawing a successful treatment during the second baseline phase to demonstrate experimental control. When a self-injurious behavior (e.g., head banging) has been successfully eliminated, withdrawing treatment solely to confirm causality raises serious ethical concerns. In such cases, the multiple baseline design is a more ethical alternative because it does not require treatment withdrawal.

von Hippel, P.T. (2005). Mean, median, and skew: Correcting a textbook rule. Journal of Statistics Education, 13(2): "In a skewed distribution, the mean is farther out in the long tail than is the median" and "the order is always from mode, to median, to mean" moving from peak toward the long tail. "The mean is pulled in the direction of the extreme scores or tail (same as the direction of the skew), and the median is between the mean and the mode." Negative skew: mean < median < mode. Positive skew: mode < median < mean.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Pearson, K. (1900). On the criterion that a given system of deviations... Philosophical Magazine, 50(302), 157-175: The chi-square statistic compares observed frequencies to expected (theoretical) frequencies, with each individual counted in one and only one category. Used to test goodness of fit for a single nominal variable.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Spearman, C. (1904). The proof and measurement of association between two things. American Journal of Psychology, 15(1), 72-101: Spearman introduced the method of "Comparison by Rank," in which the rank-order correlation substitutes ordinal rankings for actual measurements. The Spearman rank-order correlation coefficient (rho) is appropriate when both variables are measured on an ordinal scale (i.e., both sets of scores are reported as ranks).

Keselman, H.J. et al. (2001). The analysis of repeated measures designs. British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology, 54(1), 1-20: "The simplest of the between-by within-subjects RM designs involves a single between-subjects grouping factor and a single within-subjects RM factor." A mixed research design combines at least one between-subjects factor (e.g., random assignment to MBCT vs. MBSR) with at least one within-subjects factor (e.g., repeated measurements at multiple time points).

Savage, J. (2000). Ethnography and health care. BMJ, 321(7273), 1400-1402: "For many, the defining feature of ethnography is the use of participant observation, entailing prolonged fieldwork." Ethnographic research involves the study of people and their cultures through direct observation and participation, with participant observation as the hallmark method.

chapter_title: Applied Principles and the Bridge to Research Methods
verbatim: Steckler, A. & McLeroy, K.R. (2008). The importance of external validity. American Journal of Public Health, 98(1), 9-10: External validity concerns "whether causal relationships can be generalized to different measures, persons, settings, and times."

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Neyman & Pearson (1933): "We fail to reject H_0 when some alternative hypothesis is true." They called these "errors of the second kind" -- concluding there is no difference when one actually exists.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Yang, K., Tu, J., & Chen, T. (2019). Homoscedasticity: An overlooked critical assumption for linear regression. General Psychiatry, 32(5), e100148: The ANOVA model requires "constant variance sigma^2 for all subjects." Homoscedasticity means the variance of Y is approximately equal across all values of X.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Sackett, P.R. & Yang, H. (2000). Correction for range restriction: An expanded typology. Journal of Applied Psychology, 85(1), 112-118: Direct truncation on x reduces the population correlation from .50 to .33, demonstrating that restriction of range produces attenuated (smaller) observed correlations.

chapter_title: Prediction and Complexity: Regression, Multivariate Methods, and the Limits of Validity
verbatim: Bring, J. (1994). How to standardize regression coefficients. The American Statistician, 48(3), 209-213: A negative beta weight indicates an inverse relationship between that predictor and the criterion, holding all other predictors constant.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Gueorguieva, R. & Krystal, J.H. (2004). Move over ANOVA. Archives of General Psychiatry, 61(3), 310-317: Repeated-measures designs involve "multiple measurements of the same individual." "Sequential observations of the same individual tend to be closer in value to one another than the same number of observations collected from different individuals." The correlated-samples t-test compares two sets of scores from the same or matched participants.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: "The sampling distribution shows normal distribution with mean of mu and variance of sigma-squared/n." From the formula SE = sigma / sqrt(n), the standard error of the mean decreases as the population standard deviation decreases and as the sample size increases.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Kornbrot, D. (2014). Point biserial correlation. In Wiley StatsRef: "The point biserial correlation, r_pb, is the value of Pearson's product moment correlation when one of the variables is dichotomous, taking on only two possible values coded 0 and 1, and the other variable is metric (interval or ratio)."

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Mukaka, M.M. (2012). A guide to appropriate use of correlation coefficient in medical research. Malawi Medical Journal, 24(3), 69-71: Pearson's product moment correlation coefficient "represents the strength of the putative linear association between the variables in question." "It is used when both variables being studied are normally distributed." Appropriate when both variables are continuous and measured on an interval or ratio scale.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Bridge, P.D. & Sawilowsky, S.S. (1999). Increasing physicians' awareness of the impact of statistics on research outcomes. Journal of Clinical Epidemiology, 52(3), 229-235: "The t-test is one of the most prevalent tests used in the medical field and is the uniformly most powerful unbiased test (UMPU) under normal curve theory." Parametric tests are generally more powerful than nonparametric counterparts when assumptions are met.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Sawyer, S.F. (2009). Analysis of variance: The fundamental concepts. Journal of Manual & Manipulative Therapy, 17(2), 27E-38E: "F = Mean Squares_Between Groups / Mean Squares_Within Groups (Error)." "The MS_Between Groups term is an indirect measure of differences in group means" while "The MS_Within Groups (Error) term is considered to represent statistical noise/error since this variance is not explained by the effect of the independent variable." The numerator reflects treatment effects plus error; the denominator reflects error alone.

Palinkas, L.A. et al. (2015). Purposeful sampling for qualitative data collection and analysis in mixed method implementation research. Administration and Policy in Mental Health, 42(5), 533-544: In purposive (purposeful) sampling, "cases are selected because they are 'information rich' and illuminative." Researchers use their own judgment to identify and select participants who possess particular characteristics or experiences relevant to the research question, rather than relying on random selection.

chapter_title: Prediction and Complexity: Regression, Multivariate Methods, and the Limits of Validity
verbatim: Thompson, B. (1984). Canonical correlation analysis: Uses and interpretation. Sage: Canonical correlation is an extension of multiple regression that accommodates two or more predictors and two or more criteria simultaneously. It identifies linear combinations of the predictor set and criterion set that are maximally correlated, making it the appropriate multivariate technique when multiple predictors are used to estimate status on multiple criterion variables.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: "The statistical power of a significance test is the long-term probability, given the population ES, alpha, and N, of rejecting H_0. Power is thus 1 - beta." Power increases with larger alpha, larger effect size, larger sample size, and use of parametric tests.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: "The statistical power of a significance test is the long-term probability, given the population ES, alpha, and N, of rejecting H_0. Power is thus 1 - beta." Increasing alpha from .01 to .05 increases both statistical power (1 - beta) and the probability of making a Type I error, because alpha and beta are inversely related.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: In the single-sample t-test, a sample mean is compared against a known or hypothesized population mean (mu).

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Tsiatis, A.A. et al. (2008). Covariate adjustment for two-sample treatment comparisons in randomized clinical trials. Statistics in Medicine, 27(23), 4658-4677: The treatment effect beta_Z is "interpreted as the 'treatment effect after adjusting for the covariates X.'" "One goal of adjustment is to exploit covariates associated with outcome to increase precision of estimation of the treatment effect, thereby increasing statistical power." ANCOVA statistically controls for covariates, reducing error variance for a more precise estimate of treatment effects.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: "When samples are drawn from population N (mu, sigma-squared) with a sample size of n, the distribution of the sample mean should be a normal distribution." The distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as n increases, regardless of the population distribution's shape.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Pearson, K. (1900). On the criterion that a given system of deviations... Philosophical Magazine, 50(302), 157-175: The chi-square test for independence tests whether two nominal variables are related or independent across independent groups.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Pearson, K. (1900). On the criterion that a given system of deviations from the probable in the case of a correlated system of variables is such that it can be reasonably supposed to have arisen from random sampling. Philosophical Magazine, 50(302), 157-175: The chi-square test for independence (multiple-sample chi-square) tests whether the distribution of a nominal variable differs across independent groups. It compares observed to expected frequencies when each participant is classified on two categorical variables (e.g., gender and department admission).

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Sawyer, S.F. (2009). Analysis of variance: The fundamental concepts. Journal of Manual & Manipulative Therapy, 17(2), 27E-38E: "When an ANOVA has two or more independent variables it is referred to as a factorial ANOVA." For a two-factor ANOVA, "there are three Null hypotheses: (i) Main Effect for the GENDER factor... (ii) Main Effect for the TREATMENT factor... (iii) Interaction." A factorial ANOVA examines the effects of two or more independent variables on a single dependent variable, yielding main effects and an interaction effect.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Ozer, D.J. (1985). Correlation and the coefficient of determination. Psychological Bulletin, 97(2), 307-315: "This index, referred to by Guilford (1936) as the coefficient of determination, is interpreted as the percentage of variance in one variable that is predicted or explained by the other." "r^2 is the percent of determination of y by x."

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: "Paired t tests can be categorized as a type of t test for a single sample because they test the difference between two paired results." A t-test for correlated (paired) samples is used to compare the scores that subjects in two groups receive on a measure (such as symptom severity) after they receive treatment, when the subjects in the two groups have been matched on relevant variables prior to the study.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Armstrong, R.A. (2014). When to use the Bonferroni correction. Ophthalmic and Physiological Optics, 34(5), 502-508: "As the number of tests increases, so does the likelihood of a type I error." ANOVA evaluates all mean differences simultaneously, avoiding inflated experimentwise alpha from multiple comparisons.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Sawyer, S.F. (2009). Analysis of variance: The fundamental concepts. Journal of Manual & Manipulative Therapy, 17(2), 27E-38E: "F = Mean Squares_Between Groups / Mean Squares_Within Groups (Error)." "The MS_Between Groups term is an indirect measure of differences in group means" and reflects "the effects of the independent variable, or treatment, plus error." The numerator of the F-ratio (mean square between) estimates variability in dependent variable scores due to treatment effects plus error.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Cohen, J., Cohen, P., West, S.G., & Aiken, L.S. (2003). Applied multiple regression/correlation analysis for the behavioral sciences (3rd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum: The eta (correlation ratio) coefficient measures the degree of association between two variables when both are measured on a continuous (interval or ratio) scale and their relationship is nonlinear (curvilinear). Unlike Pearson's r, which assumes linearity, eta can detect curvilinear relationships.

chapter_title: Relationships in Data: Correlation and Bivariate Statistics
verbatim: Spearman, C. (1904). The proof and measurement of association between two things. American Journal of Psychology, 15(1), 72-101: Spearman introduces the method of "Comparison by Rank," applicable when measurements are ordinal rather than interval. The rank method substitutes rank order for actual measurements, and "correlation by rank" is preferable when data are ordinal.

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Lee, S. & Lee, D.K. (2018). What is the proper way to apply the multiple comparison test? Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 71(5), 353-360: "When the null hypothesis (H0) is rejected after ANOVA... we do not know how one group differs from a certain group." Post-hoc tests are conducted after a significant omnibus F to determine which specific pairs of means differ significantly, and are applicable when the independent variable has three or more levels.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Gueorguieva, R. & Krystal, J.H. (2004). Move over ANOVA. Archives of General Psychiatry, 61(3), 310-317: MANOVA analyzes group differences when there are multiple dependent variables measured at the interval/ratio level.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Keselman, H.J. et al. (2001). The analysis of repeated measures designs. British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology, 54(1), 1-20: "The simplest of the between-by within-subjects RM designs involves a single between-subjects grouping factor and a single within-subjects RM factor." A mixed-design (split-plot) ANOVA combines at least one between-subjects factor and one within-subjects factor.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Gueorguieva, R. & Krystal, J.H. (2004). Move over ANOVA. Archives of General Psychiatry, 61(3), 310-317: MANOVA (multivariate analysis of variance) is used to analyze data from studies with one or more independent variables and two or more dependent variables measured on an interval or ratio scale. By making a single multivariate comparison rather than multiple separate ANOVAs, MANOVA reduces the probability of making a Type I error (familywise error rate).

chapter_title: Prediction and Complexity: Regression, Multivariate Methods, and the Limits of Validity
verbatim: Boedeker, P. & Kearns, N.T. (2019). Linear discriminant analysis for prediction of group membership. Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 2(3), 250-263: "Fisher (1936) originally developed LDA as a method for finding linear combinations of variables that best separated observations into groups." Used "for allocating new observations to previously defined categories through the creation of a classification rule."

chapter_title: Prediction and Complexity: Regression, Multivariate Methods, and the Limits of Validity
verbatim: Whittingham, M.J. et al. (2006). Why do we still use stepwise modelling in ecology and behaviour? Journal of Animal Ecology, 75(5), 1182-1189: Stepwise multiple regression involves "algorithms that operate by successive addition or removal of significant or nonsignificant terms." "A MAM is defined as the model that contains the minimum number of predictors that satisfy some criterion." The software selects predictor variables to find the smallest subset maximizing variance explained.

Kim, T.K. (2015). T test as a parametric statistic. Korean Journal of Anesthesiology, 68(6), 540-546: Since 68% of scores fall within +/-1 SD, the remaining 32% is split equally between the two tails due to symmetry. Approximately 16% of scores fall above +1 SD and 16% below -1 SD.

Stevens, S.S. (1946). On the theory of scales of measurement. Science, 103(2684), 677-680: "Determination of equality... the scale that is just barely enough to make possible a decision of identity" characterizes nominal measurement. Nominal data categorize observations without inherent order. A bar graph is the appropriate visual display for nominal data because it uses separated bars to represent discrete categories, with bar height indicating frequency or count.

Israel, B.A. et al. (1998). Review of community-based research: Assessing partnership approaches to improve public health. Annual Review of Public Health, 19, 173-202: CBPR is "a partnership approach to research that equitably involves community members, organizational representatives, and academic researchers in all aspects of the research process." The distinguishing characteristic of CBPR is that community members function as equal research partners who participate in all phases -- from question formulation through data collection, analysis, and dissemination.

chapter_title: Prediction and Complexity: Regression, Multivariate Methods, and the Limits of Validity
verbatim: Anderson, J.C. & Gerbing, D.W. (1988). Structural equation modeling in practice. Psychological Bulletin, 103(3), 411-423: "A confirmatory measurement, or factor analysis, model specifies the relations of the observed measures to their posited underlying constructs." "A confirmatory structural model then specifies the causal relations of the constructs to one another." SEM assesses relationships among both observed (measured) variables and latent (unobserved) variables.

chapter_title: Testing Ideas: Sampling Distributions, Hypothesis Testing, and Statistical Power
verbatim: Kruschke, J.K. (2010). Bayesian data analysis. Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Cognitive Science, 1(5), 658-676: "Bayesian analysis uses Bayes' rule to combine the prior distribution with the likelihood function, yielding the posterior distribution." Bayes' theorem updates the prior probability distribution with observed data (the likelihood function) to produce a posterior (updated) probability distribution for the parameter of interest.

chapter_title: Advanced Statistical Tests: Chi-Square, MANOVA, Effect Sizes, and Clinical Significance
verbatim: Jacobson & Truax (1991): "A reliable change index (RC) is also proposed to determine whether the magnitude of change for a given client is statistically reliable." "RC tells us whether change reflects more than the fluctuations of an imprecise measuring instrument."

chapter_title: Choosing the Right Test: t-Tests, ANOVA, and Post-Hoc Comparisons
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155-159: Cohen's effect size index d = (m_A - m_B) / sigma. Because d is inversely related to sigma, greater population variability (heterogeneity) inflates sigma, shrinks d, and thereby reduces statistical power rather than increasing it.

chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Cronbach (1951): "A general formula (alpha) of which a special case is the Kuder-Richardson coefficient of equivalence is shown to be the mean of all split-half coefficients resulting from different splittings of a test." Alpha measures internal consistency -- the degree to which items consistently measure the same construct.

subdomain_name: Test Validity - Content and Construct Validity
verbatim: Kaiser, H.F. (1958). The varimax criterion for analytic rotation in factor analysis. Psychometrika, 23(3), 187-200: Orthogonal factors are constrained to be independent and uncorrelated with each other.

Harvill, L.M. (1991). Standard error of measurement. Educational Measurement: Issues and Practice, 10(2), 33-41: At the 95% confidence level, the true score falls within obtained score +/- 1.96 standard errors of measurement.

subdomain_name: Test Validity - Content and Construct Validity
chapter_title: Measuring What Matters: Construct Validity and the Multitrait-Multimethod Matrix
verbatim: Campbell & Fiske (1959): Small correlations in heterotrait-monomethod cells demonstrate that different constructs measured by the same method remain distinct -- supporting divergent validity. Both convergent and divergent evidence are necessary to establish construct validity.

Fan, X. (1998). Item response theory and classical test theory. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 58(3), 357-381: The p value "is an inverse indicator of item difficulty, with higher value indicating an easier item." Low p-values indicate difficult items; high p-values indicate easy items. To make a test easier, remove items with low p-values and add items with high p-values.

Kelley, T.L. (1939). The selection of upper and lower groups for the validation of test items. Journal of Educational Psychology, 30(1), 17-24: The item discrimination index (D) is calculated by subtracting the proportion of low-scoring examinees who answered the item correctly from the proportion of high-scoring examinees who answered correctly. D ranges from -1.0 to +1.0. A D near 0 indicates the item does not discriminate between high and low performers; a positive D indicates better discrimination; a negative D indicates the item is answered correctly more often by low scorers.

Hambleton, R.K. & Jones, R.W. (1993). Comparison of classical test theory and item response theory and their applications to test development. Educational Measurement: Issues and Practice, 12(3), 38-47: "The c parameter in the model is the height of the lower asymptote of the ICC and is introduced into the model to account for the performance of low-ability examinees on multiple-choice test items." The point at which the item characteristic curve intercepts the Y (vertical) axis provides information about the pseudo-guessing parameter -- the probability of answering the item correctly by guessing alone.

Yerushalmy, J. (1947). Statistical problems in assessing methods of medical diagnosis, with special reference to X-ray techniques. Public Health Reports, 62(40), 1432-1449: "Sensitivity would indicate the capacity for correct diagnosis in confirmed positive cases." A test developer calculates sensitivity by dividing the number of true positives identified by the test by the sum of true positives plus false negatives: Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN).

Meehl & Rosen (1955): To reduce false positives, raise the predictor cutoff score. A higher cutoff means fewer people are selected, reducing false positives but potentially increasing false negatives.

chapter_title: Predicting Outcomes: Criterion Validity, Decision Accuracy, and the Limits of Prediction
verbatim: Messick, S. (1995). Validity of psychological assessment. American Psychologist, 50(9), 741-749: When the predictor test is administered first and the criterion measure is collected at a later time, the correlation between the two constitutes predictive validity evidence.

Lord, F.M. (1952). The relation of the reliability of multiple-choice tests to the distribution of item difficulties. Psychometrika, 17(2), 181-194: For dichotomously scored items, the optimal average item difficulty level is midway between perfect performance (1.0) and the chance level of guessing. For true/false items (where chance = .50), the optimal average p = (1.0 + .50) / 2 = .75. For four-option multiple-choice items (chance = .25), optimal average p = (1.0 + .25) / 2 = .625.

Kelley, T.L. (1939). The selection of upper and lower groups for the validation of test items. Journal of Educational Psychology, 30(1), 17-24: When all examinees in the upper group answer correctly and none in the lower group do, D = +1.0 (perfect positive discrimination). When all in the lower group answer correctly and none in the upper group do, D = -1.0 (perfect negative discrimination).

Fan, X. (1998). Item response theory and classical test theory. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 58(3), 357-381: The p-value "is an inverse indicator of item difficulty, with higher value indicating an easier item." A p-value near .20 means only about 20% of test-takers answer correctly, indicating a difficult item.

Fan, X. (1998). Item response theory and classical test theory. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 58(3), 357-381: The p-value represents the proportion of people who get the item correct, ranging from 0 (no one answers correctly) to 1.0 (everyone answers correctly).

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Cortina, J.M. (1993). What is coefficient alpha? An examination of theory and applications. Journal of Applied Psychology, 78(1), 98-104: "Number of items has a profound effect on alpha, especially at low levels of average item intercorrelation." Increasing items from 6 to 18 raises alpha from .72 to .95. Additionally, a heterogeneous sample produces greater between-person variance, increasing the reliability coefficient. A split-half reliability coefficient of .75 obtained from a restricted-range sample (GPAs 3.5-4.0) would be expected to increase when the test is administered to a more heterogeneous sample (GPAs 2.0-4.0), because greater score variability inflates the proportion of true score variance relative to total variance.

subdomain_name: Test Validity - Content and Construct Validity
verbatim: Fabrigar, L.R. et al. (1999). Evaluating the use of exploratory factor analysis in psychological research. Psychological Methods, 4(3), 272-299: Communality is "the proportion of variance in a measured variable accounted for by the common factors." In a factor matrix, a communality indicates the total proportion of variability in a single test that is explained by all of the identified factors combined.

chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Novick, M.R. (1966) / Harvill, L.M. (1991): Reliability is "the ratio of between-person variance to total variance" -- the proportion of observed score variance attributable to true score variance. Reliability coefficients are interpreted directly: when the reliability coefficient is .80, this means that 80% of the variability in test scores is due to true score variability and 20% is due to measurement error.

chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Novick, M.R. (1966). The axioms and principal results of classical test theory. Journal of Mathematical Psychology, 3(1), 1-18: The classical test theory model: "X = T + E" where "X, T, and E are random variables." The observed score X is the composite of the true score T and a random error component E.

chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Novick, M.R. (1966). The axioms and principal results of classical test theory. Journal of Mathematical Psychology, 3(1), 1-18: Error scores are "uncorrelated with each other and with true scores." The error of measurement is "unsystematic, random, and due to chance."

Hambleton, R.K. & Jones, R.W. (1993). Comparison of classical test theory and item response theory. Educational Measurement: Issues and Practice, 12(3), 38-47: "The c parameter in the model is the height of the lower asymptote of the ICC and is introduced into the model to account for the performance of low-ability examinees on multiple-choice test items." The pseudo-guessing parameter represents the probability of correct response by guessing alone, corresponding to the y-axis intercept of the item characteristic curve.

chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Harvill, L.M. (1991). Standard error of measurement. Educational Measurement: Issues and Practice, 10(2), 33-41: "SEM = S_X sqrt(1 - r_xx)." "In order to get an estimate of the SEM, subtract the test reliability from one, take the square root of that difference, and multiply the square root value times the standard deviation of the test scores." Computing SEM requires the standard deviation and the reliability coefficient.

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Kuder, G.F. & Richardson, M.W. (1937). The theory of the estimation of test reliability. chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Eisinga, R. et al. (2013). The reliability of a two-item scale. chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Kazdin, A.E. (1977). Artifact, bias, and complexity of assessment: The ABCs of reliability. chapter_title: Building Consistent Measures: Classical Test Theory and the Foundations of Reliability
verbatim: Cortina, J.M. (1993). What is coefficient alpha? An examination of theory and applications. Journal of Applied Psychology, 78(1), 98-104: "Number of items has a profound effect on alpha, especially at low levels of average item intercorrelation." More items increase reliability. Additionally, a heterogeneous sample of examinees produces greater between-person variability, which increases the ratio of true score variance to total variance and thus increases the reliability coefficient. chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Cohen, J. (1960). A coefficient of agreement for nominal scales. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 20(1), 37-46: "A certain amount of agreement is to be expected by chance." Percent agreement is misleading because it does not correct for chance agreement.

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency

verbatim: Kaiser, H.F. (1958). The varimax criterion for analytic rotation in factor analysis. Psychometrika, 23(3), 187-200: Varimax is an orthogonal rotation method; the resulting factors are uncorrelated.

chapter_title: Reliability in Practice: Methods, Agreement, and the Factors That Shape Consistency
verbatim: Cortina, J.M. (1993). What is coefficient alpha? A more heterogeneous sample produces greater between-person variance, also increasing the reliability coefficient.

verbatim: Fabrigar, L.R. et al. (1999). Evaluating the use of exploratory factor analysis in psychological research. verbatim: Campbell & Fiske (1959): The MTMM organizes convergent and discriminant validity evidence. Tests measuring the same construct should correlate highly (convergent), and the construct should not correlate highly with different constructs (discriminant).

verbatim: Fabrigar, L.R. et al. (1999). Evaluating the use of exploratory factor analysis in psychological research. verbatim: Cohen, J. (1960). A coefficient of agreement for nominal scales. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 20(1), 37-46: "Kappa is simply the portion of agreement corrected for chance." Yerushalmy, J. (1947). Statistical problems in assessing methods of medical diagnosis, with special reference to X-ray techniques. verbatim: Piaget (1964): "To know an object is to act on it. To know is to modify, to transform the object, and to understand the process of this transformation." Assimilation incorporates new information into existing schemas; accommodation modifies schemas in response to information that doesn't fit. For example, a child who applies the word "doggie" to all furry four-legged animals is demonstrating assimilation -- fitting new experiences into an existing schema.

verbatim: Piaget (1962): Deferred imitation and make-believe play emerge during the sixth and final substage of the sensorimotor period, at approximately 18 months of age. These abilities reflect the child's capacity to create mental representations -- internal images of objects and events that can be recalled and acted upon in the absence of the original stimulus. The emergence of representational thought marks the transition from sensorimotor to preoperational intelligence.

verbatim: Elkind (1967): The personal fable is the adolescent belief in one's uniqueness and invulnerability -- that rules and consequences applying to others do not apply to oneself. The bump is attributed to identity formation, novelty of experiences, and emotional significance during this period.

chapter_title: Logic Takes Hold: Conservation, Moral Reasoning, and the Competent Child
verbatim: Piaget (1968): "The notion of substance is attained before either weight or volume... conservation of substance is simply a logical necessity." chapter_title: Words in the World: How Language Meets Social Context
verbatim: Vygotsky (1978): "The zone of proximal development is the distance between the actual developmental level as determined by independent problem solving and the level of potential development as determined through problem solving under adult guidance or in collaboration with more capable peers." The ZPD defines the range between what a child can do alone and what they can achieve with support.

chapter_title: Words in the World: How Language Meets Social Context

chapter_title: Logic Takes Hold: Conservation, Moral Reasoning, and the Competent Child

Stage 6 involves self-chosen ethical principles transcending specific laws.

chapter_title: Thinking in Symbols: Preoperational Cognition, Play, and the Growing Brain

chapter_title: Thinking in Symbols: Preoperational Cognition, Play, and the Growing Brain
verbatim: Lillard, Lerner, Hopkins, Dore, Smith, & Palmquist (2013): "For Vygotsky (1978), pretense has a crucial developmental role, because it is the activity by which children learn to separate referent from object... children also develop inhibitory control through pretend play (Bodrova & Leong, 1996)." verbatim: Hasher, Zacks, & May (1999): "The synchrony effect refers to the finding that cognitive performance is optimal when testing occurs at one's circadian peak: older adults perform best in the morning, younger adults in the late afternoon/evening." chapter_title: How Minds Begin: Piaget's Sensorimotor Stage and the Construction of Infant Knowledge
verbatim: Piaget (1964) / Berk (2018): "Assimilation is the process of incorporating new information into an existing cognitive schema without modifying the schema." When a child interprets the moon's apparent movement as 'following' them, the child is assimilating this observation into the existing schema that things that move alongside you are following you.

chapter_title: The First Words: How Infants Build a Language from Sound and Meaning
verbatim: Brown & Bellugi (1964): Children's speech preserves the word order of adult models but omits functors — inflections, auxiliary verbs, articles, prepositions, and conjunctions. Free morphemes can stand alone as words (e.g., 'do'); bound morphemes cannot stand alone and must attach to another morpheme (e.g., 'pre-,' '-ism').

verbatim: Chomsky (1959/1965): Syntax specifies how words combine to form grammatically correct sentences, including word order and relationships between elements. verbatim: Sneed, Whitbourne, & Culang (2006): Erikson's industry vs. inferiority stage spans ages 6-11, during which children turn energy toward mastering culturally valued knowledge and skills. chapter_title: The Storm and the Search: Puberty, Identity, and Adolescent Cognition

chapter_title: The First Words: How Infants Build a Language from Sound and Meaning
verbatim: Brown (1973) / Berk (2018): "Morphemes are the smallest meaningful units of language. verbatim: Dugas, Gagnon, Ladouceur, & Freeston (1998), *Journal of Anxiety Disorders*, 12(3), 211-226: Within the intolerance of uncertainty (IU) model of GAD, pathological worry is sustained by four cognitive processes. These are: intolerance of uncertainty, defined as a disposition to respond negatively to uncertain circumstances irrespective of how likely they are; positive beliefs about worry, namely the conviction that worrying wards off bad outcomes; negative problem orientation, which involves framing problems as threats while doubting one's own problem-solving capacity; and cognitive avoidance, whereby abstract verbal-linguistic worry is used to sidestep more distressing mental imagery.

Robey (2011): "Choice Theory explains human behavior and motivation (Glasser, 1998)." In reality therapy, the primary source of human motivation is the drive to satisfy five basic innate needs: survival (physical safety and sustenance), love and belonging (connection and affiliation), power (competence, achievement, and recognition), freedom (autonomy and independence), and fun (enjoyment and learning).

Acceptance involves the active and aware embrace of those private events without unnecessary attempts to change their frequency or form.

chapter_title: Before the Crisis: Prevention Science, Research Bias, and the Early Architecture of Therapeutic Change
