chapter_title: Transforming Organizations: Change Models, OD Interventions, and the Art of Continuous Improvement
verbatim: Lewin (1951): "Lewin's three-stage model of planned organizational change involves unfreezing (disrupting the status quo), changing (introducing new behaviors or processes), and refreezing (stabilizing the new state as the norm)."

chapter_title: Transforming Organizations: Change Models, OD Interventions, and the Art of Continuous Improvement
verbatim: Deming (1986): "Total quality management (TQM) centers on continuous improvement, full employee involvement, customer satisfaction, and data-driven decision-making as organizational operating principles."

chapter_title: Transforming Organizations: Change Models, OD Interventions, and the Art of Continuous Improvement
verbatim: Cooperrider & Whitney (2005): The 4D model of appreciative inquiry describes organizational change as a cycle of four stages: Discovery (appreciating "the best of what is"), Dream (envisioning "what might be"), Design (co-constructing "what should be"), and Destiny (sustaining "what will be"). The first stage, Discovery, involves identifying and appreciating the organization's existing strengths and peak experiences.

chapter_title: Transforming Organizations: Change Models, OD Interventions, and the Art of Continuous Improvement
verbatim: Ishikawa (1985): "Quality circles are small voluntary groups of employees who meet regularly to identify, analyze, and propose solutions for work-related problems, presenting recommendations to management."
Baltes et al. (1999): "The compressed workweek shows a strong positive effect on employee job satisfaction, a weaker but positive effect on productivity, and inconclusive effects on absenteeism."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Hersey & Blanchard (1977): "Situational leadership theory recommends a telling (high task/low relationship) style for subordinates who have both low ability and low motivation."

Judge, Piccolo, & Ilies (2004): "High consideration (person-orientation) is most strongly associated with subordinate satisfaction, while high initiating structure (task-orientation) is most strongly associated with group performance."

chapter_title: Defining the Work: Job Analysis Methods and the Foundations of Fair Compensation
verbatim:
McCormick (1979): "Job analysis methods fall into three categories: work-oriented (focusing on tasks and duties), worker-oriented (focusing on human attributes needed), or hybrid approaches combining both perspectives."

chapter_title: Developing Talent: Training, Evaluation, Workplace Well-Being, and the Integration of Career Science
verbatim: Baltes, Briggs, Huff, Wright, & Neuman (1999): "Among the effects of the compressed workweek, meta-analytic evidence indicates the strongest beneficial effect is on job satisfaction, with weaker positive effects on productivity and mixed findings regarding absenteeism." Context: The compressed workweek allows employees to work their full weekly hours in fewer days (e.g., four 10-hour days), a schedule arrangement most strongly associated with attitudinal benefits.

Judge, Piccolo, & Ilies (2004): "A high level of consideration (person-oriented leadership behavior) is more strongly associated with subordinate satisfaction, while a high level of initiating structure (task-oriented leadership behavior) is more strongly associated with group and organizational performance." Context: These two dimensions, identified in the Ohio State leadership studies, are independent -- a leader can be high or low on either or both.
Judge, Bono, Ilies, & Gerhardt (2002): "Among the Big Five personality traits, extraversion and conscientiousness are the strongest predictors of leader effectiveness."

chapter_title: Finding the Right People: Selection Methods and What Predicts Job Success
verbatim: Schmidt & Hunter (1998): "Combining a general mental ability test with an integrity test produces the largest gain in predictive validity for job performance, as these two measures tap distinct performance determinants."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Hersey & Blanchard (1977): "Hersey and Blanchard's situational leadership theory prescribes a selling (high task/high relationship) style for followers who have some competence but lack full commitment or confidence."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Fiedler (1967): "Fiedler's contingency theory holds that low-LPC (task-oriented) leaders are most effective in situations of very high or very low situational control, while high-LPC (relationship-oriented) leaders are most effective under moderate situational control."

Bass (1985): "Transformational leadership comprises four components: idealized influence (serving as a role model), inspirational motivation (articulating a compelling vision), intellectual stimulation (encouraging creative problem-solving), and individualized consideration (attending to each follower's needs)."
Eagly, Karau, & Makhijani (1995): "Female leaders receive higher effectiveness ratings in work contexts viewed as feminine, while male leaders receive higher ratings in contexts viewed as masculine."

Fiedler (1967): "Low LPC leaders are task-oriented and describe their least preferred coworker in negative terms because they evaluate coworkers primarily through the lens of task performance, while high LPC leaders are relationship-oriented and describe their least preferred coworker in relatively positive terms because they separate interpersonal qualities from task competence." Context: The Least Preferred Coworker (LPC) scale is Fiedler's measure of leadership orientation, with the score reflecting the leader's primary motivational hierarchy.

Fleishman (1953): "The Ohio State University leadership studies identified two independent dimensions of leader behavior: initiating structure (the extent to which a leader defines and organizes roles, establishes channels of communication, and focuses on goal attainment) and consideration (the extent to which a leader shows mutual trust, respect, and concern for subordinates' welfare)." Context: These two dimensions emerged from factor analysis of the Leader Behavior Description Questionnaire (LBDQ) and established that leadership behaviors could be measured along independent task-oriented and people-oriented dimensions.

Bass & Avolio (1995): "The Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ) assesses two transactional leadership dimensions: contingent reward (exchanging rewards for performance) and management-by-exception (intervening when standards are not met)."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Graen & Uhl-Bien (1995): "Leader-member exchange (LMX) theory proposes that leaders form qualitatively different relationships with subordinates, distinguishing between in-group members (who receive greater trust, resources, and latitude) and out-group members."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Janis (1982): "Groupthink is most likely to develop in highly cohesive groups with a strong directive leader who signals a preferred course of action, combined with insulation from outside expert opinion."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Simon (1957): "Simon's bounded rationality model holds that decision-makers satisfice -- selecting the first minimally acceptable alternative rather than optimizing -- due to cognitive limitations, time constraints, and incomplete information."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Vroom & Yetton (1973): "The Vroom-Yetton-Jago normative decision model provides a decision tree to determine the optimal degree of subordinate participation in decision-making, ranging from autocratic to fully democratic styles."

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: House (1971): Path-goal theory proposes that a leader's effectiveness depends on matching leadership style to subordinate and task characteristics. The theory identifies four leadership styles (directive, supportive, participative, achievement-oriented). An advocate of path-goal theory would assess characteristics of the supervisee (e.g., experience, locus of control) and the job tasks (e.g., structure, complexity) and, based on those factors, recommend adopting the most appropriate style -- such as a supportive style when the supervisee faces stressful or dissatisfying tasks.

chapter_title: Leading People: Contingency, Situational, and Relational Approaches to Leadership
verbatim: Vroom & Yetton (1973): "The Vroom-Yetton-Jago contingency model provides a decision tree in which leaders answer a series of diagnostic questions about the decision situation, leading to a recommended leadership style that ranges from highly autocratic (the leader decides alone) to highly democratic (the group makes the decision by consensus)." Context: The model was later revised by Vroom and Jago (1988) to incorporate additional situational factors and continuous rather than discrete scales.

chapter_title: What Drives Us: Content Theories of Motivation and Selection Decision Models
verbatim: Hackman & Oldham (1976): The job characteristics model identifies five core job dimensions: (1) skill variety -- the degree to which a job requires different skills, (2) task identity -- completing a whole piece of work, (3) task significance -- impact on others, (4) autonomy -- freedom in scheduling and methods, and (5) feedback -- direct information about performance effectiveness. These five dimensions create three critical psychological states that drive internal work motivation.

chapter_title: What Drives Us: Content Theories of Motivation and Selection Decision Models
verbatim: Herzberg (1966): "Herzberg's two-factor theory proposes that job satisfaction and motivation are increased by intrinsic motivators (autonomy, responsibility, advancement, recognition) rather than by hygiene factors (pay, working conditions, supervision), which only prevent dissatisfaction."

Simon (1957): "Simon identified cognitive limitations, time constraints, and imperfect or inadequate information as the causes of satisficing -- the decision-making strategy of considering alternatives only until a minimally acceptable option is found rather than exhaustively searching for the optimal solution." Context: Satisficing is the practical consequence of bounded rationality, reflecting the reality that decision-makers in organizations operate under constraints that prevent fully rational optimization.

Locke & Latham (2002): "Research on goal-setting theory has found that when employees participate in setting their own performance goals, the goals they set tend to be more difficult than those the supervisor would have assigned unilaterally." Context: Participative goal-setting can enhance commitment and lead to higher goals, although assigned goals can be equally effective when accepted by the employee.

Judge, Bono, Ilies, & Gerhardt (2002): "Among the Big Five traits, extraversion is the single strongest predictor of leader effectiveness, followed by conscientiousness, openness, and low neuroticism."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Isenberg (1986): "Group polarization is said to occur when an initial tendency of individual group members toward a given direction is enhanced following group discussion. On decisions in which group members have, on the average, a moderate proclivity in a given direction, group discussion results in a more extreme average proclivity in the same direction."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Janis (1982): "Appointing a designated devil's advocate to challenge assumptions and present counterarguments is an established strategy for reducing the likelihood of groupthink."

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Van Eerde & Thierry (1996): "This meta-analysis integrates the correlations of 77 studies on V. H. Vroom's (1964) original expectancy models and work-related criteria." Vroom's expectancy theory identifies three key components -- valence (the value placed on outcomes), instrumentality (the belief that performance leads to outcomes), and expectancy (the belief that effort leads to performance). To alleviate low motivation using this framework, an employer would ask employees about their expectancy, instrumentality, and valence -- but not about commitment, which is not a component of Vroom's model.

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Colquitt (2001): "Distributive justice refers to perceived fairness in the allocation of organizational outcomes (e.g., pay, promotions, bonuses)."

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Vroom (1964): "In expectancy theory, expectancy refers to an individual's subjective belief that exerting a given level of effort will lead to successful task performance, expressed as a probability ranging from 0 to 1." Context: Expectancy is one of three cognitive components in Vroom's model, alongside instrumentality (belief that performance leads to outcomes) and valence (value placed on those outcomes).

Judge, Thoresen, Bono, & Patton (2001): "The satisfaction-performance relationship is strongest when pay and other reinforcers are closely contingent on performance, supporting a reciprocal rather than unidirectional causal model."

Colquitt, Conlon, Wesson, Porter, & Ng (2001): "The authors conducted a meta-analytic review of 183 justice studies. The results suggest that although different justice dimensions are moderately to highly related, they contribute incremental variance explained in fairness perceptions."

Brockner (1988): "Downsizing survivor syndrome -- characterized by reduced trust, motivation, and organizational commitment -- is attributed to perceived violation of the psychological contract: the unwritten mutual expectations between employees and the organization."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Janis (1982): "To reduce the risk of groupthink, the group leader should remain neutral at the beginning of discussions, actively encourage members to express dissenting opinions, appoint one member to serve as devil's advocate, and invite outside experts to provide independent perspectives." Context: These preventive strategies counteract the antecedent conditions of groupthink by disrupting premature consensus, reducing conformity pressure, and introducing diverse viewpoints.

chapter_title: What Drives Us: Content Theories of Motivation and Selection Decision Models
verbatim: Maslow (1943): "Maslow's hierarchy of needs predicts sequential motivation: once physiological and safety needs are met, the individual is primarily motivated by social (belongingness) needs before esteem and self-actualization needs emerge."

chapter_title: What Drives Us: Content Theories of Motivation and Selection Decision Models
verbatim: Herzberg (1966): "Job enrichment -- redesigning work to incorporate greater responsibility, challenge, and growth opportunities -- is the primary strategy for increasing intrinsic motivation and satisfaction."
Maslach, Schaufeli, & Leiter (2001): "An early indicator of job burnout is increased time and effort invested in work tasks without a corresponding increase in productivity."
Faragher, Cass, & Cooper (2005): "Job satisfaction relates to both mental and physical health outcomes, with a stronger association for mental health."

chapter_title: Making Better Decisions: Bounded Rationality, Groupthink, and When Groups Go Wrong
verbatim: Simon (1957): "Managers often satisfice rather than optimize or maximize in organizational decision-making because cognitive limitations, time constraints, and incomplete information prevent them from identifying and evaluating all possible alternatives to find the absolute best solution." Context: Simon's concept of bounded rationality challenged classical economic models that assumed fully rational decision-makers, and the tendency to satisfice is a direct consequence of these cognitive and situational bounds.

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Adams (1965): "Equity theory predicts that worker motivation depends on perceived fairness, determined by comparing one's own input-to-outcome ratio against the ratios of comparable others performing similar work."

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Adams (1965): "An employer applying equity theory would focus on employees' perceptions of the relative fairness of their treatment at work -- specifically, whether they believe their ratio of inputs (effort, skill, experience) to outcomes (pay, recognition, benefits) is equitable compared to the ratios of comparable others." Context: Perceived inequity, whether underpayment or overpayment, generates motivational tension that the employee seeks to reduce through behavioral or cognitive adjustments.

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Adams (1965): "Equity theory predicts that a worker's motivation is affected by the worker's perception of the degree to which he or she is being treated fairly relative to other workers, based on a comparison of input-to-outcome ratios." Context: When workers perceive inequity -- either underpayment or overpayment relative to referent others -- they experience distress and are motivated to restore equity through behavioral or cognitive means.
Staw, Bell, & Clausen (1986): "Individual levels of job satisfaction remain relatively stable over time and across different positions and career changes, suggesting a dispositional component."

Meyer et al. (2002): "Among the three forms of organizational commitment -- normative (sense of obligation), affective (emotional attachment), and continuance (perceived cost of leaving) -- affective commitment shows the strongest and most consistent correlations with desirable job outcomes."

chapter_title: The Mechanics of Motivation: Process Theories, Equity, and the Beliefs That Drive Effort
verbatim: Vroom (1964): "According to expectancy theory, an employee's motivation is determined by three beliefs: expectancy (the belief that effort will lead to successful performance), instrumentality (the belief that successful performance will lead to certain outcomes), and valence (the value the employee places on those outcomes)." Context: Motivation in Vroom's model is calculated as the product of expectancy, instrumentality, and valence; if any component is zero, overall motivation will be zero.

chapter_title: Developing Talent: Training, Evaluation, Workplace Well-Being, and the Integration of Career Science
verbatim: Sapolsky (1996): "Chronic elevation of cortisol associated with prolonged stress is linked to hippocampal neuronal loss and impaired memory consolidation."

Selye (1956): "Selye's general adaptation syndrome describes three sequential stages of the physiological stress response: alarm reaction (sympathetic activation), resistance (sustained coping with ongoing stressor), and exhaustion (depletion of adaptive resources)."
Miller et al. (1996): "Among Type A behavior pattern components, hostility is the characteristic most strongly associated with elevated cardiovascular disease risk."

Kobasa (1979): "Kobasa's construct of psychological hardiness comprises three protective dispositions -- control (belief in personal influence over events), commitment (engagement with life activities), and challenge (viewing change as opportunity rather than threat)."
Colquitt (2001): "Procedural justice refers to perceived fairness of the processes and methods used by an organization to make decisions about resource allocation, promotions, and rewards."

chapter_title: Developing Talent: Training, Evaluation, Workplace Well-Being, and the Integration of Career Science
verbatim: W. K. Kellogg Foundation (2004): "A program logic model is a visual schematic depicting the causal chain from program resources (inputs) through activities and outputs to short- and long-term outcomes, used to guide program development, implementation, and evaluation."

chapter_title: Optimizing Selection: Fairness, Legal Defenses, and Advanced Decision Problems
verbatim: Griggs v. Duke Power Co. (1971): "When a selection test is shown to produce adverse impact, the employer may continue using it only by demonstrating that the measured attributes are job-related and that no equally valid alternative with less adverse impact is available."

Eagly, Johannesen-Schmidt, & van Engen (2003): "Women tend toward transformational leadership and transactional contingent reward, while men more frequently employ transactional management-by-exception (both active and passive)."

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: McGeoch & McDonald (1931): Retroactive interference occurs when newly learned material impairs recall of previously learned material. Participants who learned a second list of words showed significantly poorer recall of the original list compared to a rest-only control group, establishing that interpolated learning — not mere passage of time — is the primary cause of forgetting.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Baddeley (2000): Working memory has four components: (1) central executive — attentional control system; (2) phonological loop — auditory-verbal information; (3) visuospatial sketchpad — visual and spatial representations; (4) episodic buffer — integrates across subsystems and long-term memory. No "semantic register" exists in the model.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Pyc & Rawson (2010): The mediator effectiveness hypothesis — the testing effect occurs because retrieval practice prompts learners to generate and strengthen mediators (semantic links) that are more effective for later recall than those established during restudy.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Rundus (1971): The primacy effect is attributable to differential rehearsal: early list items receive more rehearsals due to less competition for rehearsal time. Strong positive correlation between number of rehearsals and recall probability, confirming greater transfer into long-term memory.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Craik & Lockhart (1972): "Trace persistence is a function of depth of analysis, with deeper levels of analysis associated with more elaborate, longer lasting, and stronger traces." Elaborative rehearsal engaging deep semantic processing is far more effective than maintenance rehearsal (rote repetition).

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Baddeley (2003): "The model of the phonological loop comprises a phonological store, which can hold memory traces for a few seconds before they fade, and an articulatory rehearsal process that is analogous to subvocal speech."

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Tolman & Honzik (1930): Rats exploring a maze without reward showed sudden, dramatic improvement when food was introduced, immediately matching rewarded rats' performance. Learning had occurred but remained "latent" until motivation was introduced. Evidence of "cognitive maps" formed during unrewarded exploration.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Bandura (1977): "Fortunately, most human behavior is learned observationally through modeling: from observing others one forms an idea of how new behaviors are performed, and on later occasions this coded information serves as a guide for action."

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Peterson & Peterson (1959): When rehearsal is prevented, retention of a consonant trigram declines rapidly to near zero after approximately 18-20 seconds. Established the transient nature of short-term memory without active rehearsal.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Yates (1966): The method of loci is a mnemonic strategy in which each item to be remembered is mentally placed at a distinct location along a well-known spatial route. During retrieval, one mentally "walks" through the route, and each location serves as a retrieval cue for the associated item. Effective for encoding and recalling lists of unrelated words.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Miller (1956): "the span of immediate memory imposes severe limitations on the amount of information that we are able to receive, process, and remember," approximately seven items (plus or minus two). The capacity unit is the "chunk" — a meaningful unit that can range from a digit to a complex phrase.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Roediger & McDermott (1995): DRM paradigm — lists of semantically associated words cause frequent recall and recognition of a nonpresented critical lure with high confidence. False memories are subjectively indistinguishable from veridical memories.

Kohler (1925): Insight learning — chimpanzees, after apparent contemplation, suddenly perceived relationships among problem elements and arrived at a solution without trial-and-error. Solutions appeared as a sudden "aha" moment.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Tulving (1972): Episodic memory — "memory for personally experienced events, situated in a particular time and place." Semantic memory — "organized knowledge about the world, including facts, concepts, and word meanings, that is independent of any specific episode of learning." Both are declarative memory accessible to conscious recollection.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Tulving & Thomson (1973): "specific encoding operations performed on what is perceived determine what is stored, and what is stored determines what retrieval cues are effective in providing access to what is stored." Forgetting often reflects a mismatch between retrieval cues and the encoded trace rather than decay or storage failure.

Murdock (1962): Serial position effect: early (primacy) and late (recency) items recalled better than middle items. When a delay with distractor is inserted, the recency effect is eliminated but primacy persists — confirming early items transferred to long-term memory through greater rehearsal while recency items depend on short-term storage.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Einstein & McDaniel (1990): Prospective memory is "memory for intentions — remembering to carry out an action at some point in the future." Requires self-initiated retrieval.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Sperling (1960): Iconic (visual) sensory memory has much larger capacity than previously believed but decays within approximately 250-500 milliseconds. Echoic (auditory) sensory memory persists approximately 3-4 seconds with similarly large capacity and rapid decay.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Baddeley (2000): The episodic buffer is a limited-capacity component of working memory that "provides temporary storage of information held in a multimodal code, which is capable of binding information from the subsidiary systems, and from long-term memory, into a unitary episodic representation." It is the fourth component of the multi-component model alongside the central executive, phonological loop, and visuospatial sketchpad.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Jenkins & Dallenbach (1924): Participants who slept during the retention interval recalled significantly more than those who remained awake. Superior retention after sleep supports interference-based accounts of forgetting rather than trace decay.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Baddeley (2000): The episodic buffer integrates information across the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and long-term memory into coherent episodic representations. It is controlled by the central executive and serves as the interface between working memory subsystems and long-term memory stores.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Underwood (1957): Proactive interference (PI) — impairment of new learning by previously acquired material — is a major source of forgetting.

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Tolman & Honzik (1930): Rats that explored a maze without reinforcement showed no improvement in performance until a food reward was introduced, at which point their error rate dropped immediately to match that of consistently rewarded rats. This demonstrated that learning had occurred during unrewarded exploration but remained latent until motivation was provided — confirming that learning can occur without being apparent in behavior.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Baddeley (2000): The episodic buffer is "a limited capacity system that provides temporary storage of information held in a multimodal code, which is capable of binding information from the subsidiary systems, and from long-term memory, into a unitary episodic representation."

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: McGeoch & McDonald (1931): Retroactive interference (RI) — impairment of previously learned material by subsequently learned material. Participants learning a second list showed significantly poorer recall of the first list compared to those who rested.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Bellezza (1981): An acrostic is a mnemonic device in which the first letter of each word in a sentence corresponds to the first letter of each item to be remembered. "On old Olympus' towering top, a Finn and German viewed some hops" is a classic acrostic for the twelve cranial nerves (olfactory, optic, oculomotor, trochlear, trigeminal, abducens, facial, auditory/vestibulocochlear, glossopharyngeal, vagus, spinal accessory, hypoglossal).

chapter_title: How Memory Fails: Forgetting, Interference, and False Memory
verbatim: Peterson & Peterson (1959): The distractor task paradigm — participants study material then perform an interpolated task (counting backward) before recall. Prevents rehearsal and introduces potentially interfering information. Standard procedure for studying retroactive interference.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Atkinson (1975): The keyword method is a two-stage process: (1) "acoustic link" — an English word that sounds like part of the foreign word; (2) "imagery link" — a vivid mental image associating the keyword with the translation. Significantly enhanced vocabulary learning compared to rote rehearsal.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Yates (1966): The method of loci involves selecting a well-known spatial route and mentally placing each item at successive locations. During retrieval, one mentally "walks" through the route, each location serving as a retrieval cue.

chapter_title: Memory Architecture: From Sensory Store to Long-Term Memory
verbatim: Schacter (1987): Implicit memory is "revealed when previous experiences facilitate performance on a task that does not require conscious or intentional recollection of those experiences." Priming is a key paradigm for investigating implicit memory. Types of priming include repetition priming, in which prior presentation of a word or object facilitates later identification of that same item in a different format or context. Common repetition priming tasks include word-stem completion and word-fragment completion.

chapter_title: How We Learn and Remember: Encoding, Retrieval, and Mnemonics
verbatim: Tulving & Thomson (1973): The encoding specificity principle states that "specific encoding operations performed on what is perceived determine what is stored, and what is stored determines what retrieval cues are effective in providing access to what is stored." Retrieval from long-term memory is maximized when conditions at recall match the conditions present during encoding.

chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Allen et al. (2014): Pramipexole and ropinirole are dopamine receptor agonists used to treat restless legs syndrome (RLS). Both medications stimulate dopamine receptors directly and are first-line pharmacological treatments for moderate to severe RLS symptoms.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Purves et al. (2018): The medulla oblongata, the most caudal structure of the brainstem, contains vital autonomic centers that regulate respiration, heart rate, blood pressure, and reflexes such as swallowing, coughing, and vomiting. Damage to the medulla is frequently fatal because these life-sustaining functions are disrupted.

Sanes & Lichtman (1999): At the neuromuscular junction, "the release of the neurotransmitter, acetylcholine, is quantal and vesicular" and "the first neurotransmitter receptor to be purified and then molecularly cloned was the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (AChR)." ACh initiates muscle contraction. Myasthenia gravis involves antibodies attacking nicotinic ACh receptors. Curare is a competitive antagonist.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Moore (1997): The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus is the master circadian pacemaker in mammals, generating and coordinating approximately 24-hour cycles. It regulates the sleep-wake cycle, daily fluctuations in core body temperature, and rhythmic secretion of hormones including melatonin and cortisol via projections to the pineal gland and hypothalamic-pituitary axis.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Lazarus (1991): Cognitive appraisal theory distinguishes three types of appraisal. Primary appraisal evaluates whether an event is irrelevant, benign-positive, or stressful. Secondary appraisal involves evaluating one's available coping resources and options — what personal and environmental resources a person has to manage a stressful event. Reappraisal is the ongoing reassessment of both the event and one's coping capacity.
Gibson (1979): Bottom-up processing begins with elementary sensory features and proceeds hierarchically to pattern recognition and object identification without influence from prior knowledge.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Geschwind (1965): Conduction aphasia is a disconnection syndrome resulting from damage to the arcuate fasciculus, the white matter tract connecting Wernicke's area to Broca's area. It is characterized by fluent but paraphasic spontaneous speech, relatively intact auditory comprehension, markedly impaired repetition, and impaired naming (anomia).

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Adolphs, Tranel, Damasio, & Damasio (1994): Patient S.M., with bilateral amygdala damage due to Urbach-Wiethe disease, showed a selective impairment in recognizing fear from facial expressions while recognition of other emotions was relatively preserved. Bilateral amygdala lesions produce difficulty recognizing negative emotions, especially fear, in facial expressions.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Pattinson (2008): "opioid-induced respiratory depression is primarily mediated by mu-opioid receptors located in the brainstem respiratory centers, particularly within the medulla oblongata." The medulla's respiratory centers are inhibited by mu receptor binding, leading to decreased respiratory rate and, at toxic doses, fatal respiratory arrest.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Davis (1992): Electrical stimulation of the central nucleus of the amygdala elicits the full constellation of conditioned fear responses, including freezing behavior, increased heart rate and blood pressure, enhanced startle reflex, and release of stress hormones (corticosterone/cortisol). The amygdala is the critical structure for the acquisition and expression of conditioned fear.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Schachter & Singer (1962): The two-factor (cognitive arousal) theory proposes that emotional experience results from two components: (1) physiological arousal in response to an external event, and (2) a cognitive label or attribution for that arousal based on environmental cues. Physiological arousal is assumed to be similar across emotions; the subjective experience of different emotions depends on the cognitive interpretation of the arousal.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Howes & Kapur (2009): The dopamine hypothesis attributes psychosis in schizophrenia to hyperdopaminergia in the mesolimbic dopamine pathway, producing aberrant salience.

The Huntington's Disease Collaborative Research Group (1993): Huntington's disease follows autosomal dominant inheritance. Each child of an affected parent has 50% probability of inheriting the allele.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Lydiard (2003): "GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter in the mammalian central nervous system, and GABAergic dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety disorders." Reduced GABA levels are associated with heightened anxiety.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Geschwind (1975): Apraxia is "an inability to carry out learned, purposeful movements, despite having the physical ability and desire to perform them." Not attributable to weakness, sensory loss, incoordination, or failure to comprehend. For example, a person who knows the goal of removing a cork from a wine bottle but cannot perform the required motor sequence despite intact motor ability would be exhibiting apraxia.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Stuss & Alexander (2000): Frontal lobe damage often has an adverse effect on motivation, judgment, and memory but not on standard IQ test scores. This is because frontal lobe damage impairs divergent thinking (generating novel ideas and flexible problem-solving) but not convergent thinking (arriving at a single correct answer), which is what standard IQ tests primarily measure. Frontal lobe patients show relatively preserved IQ test performance despite marked real-world impairments.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Roffwarg, Muzio, & Dement (1966): "The neonate sleeps approximately 16-18 hours per day, with REM sleep comprising approximately 50% of total sleep time. REM sleep proportion declines steeply during the first years of life, reaching approximately 25-30% by age 5, and then remains relatively stable at 20-25% throughout adulthood."

Wandell (1995): "cone photoreceptors mediate photopic (daylight) vision, color perception, and fine spatial acuity." Rods cannot discriminate wavelengths and cannot mediate color or high-acuity central vision.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
verbatim: Saxena, Brody, Schwartz, & Baxter (1998): OCD is associated with hyperactivity in the basal ganglia, as demonstrated by functional neuroimaging.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Kopelman, Thomson, Guerrini, & Marshall (2009): The Korsakoff syndrome is a preventable memory disorder resulting from thiamine deficiency. "Lesions in the mammillary bodies, the mammillo-thalamic tract and the anterior thalamus may be more important to memory dysfunction."

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Shahrestani, Kemp, & Guastella (2013): Exogenous oxytocin administration in healthy adults without baseline deficits in emotion perception can enhance sensitivity to emotional facial expressions, potentially leading to oversensitivity. Oxytocin modulates social cognition by increasing attentional salience of social cues, including emotional faces.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Shin, Rauch, & Pitman (2006): PTSD neuroimaging shows three consistent findings: (1) amygdala hyperresponsivity to threat stimuli, (2) diminished ventromedial prefrontal cortex activation, and (3) reduced hippocampal volume.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Squire (2004): Nondeclarative (implicit) memory — procedural/skill learning, habit formation, classical conditioning, and priming — depends on structures distinct from the hippocampal system. Procedural learning depends on the basal ganglia; conditioned motor responses depend on the cerebellum. Preserved in amnesic patients.

Lipton & Rosenberg (1994): "In many neurologic disorders, injury to neurons may be caused at least in part by overstimulation of receptors for excitatory amino acids, including glutamate and aspartate." Excitotoxicity may constitute "a final common pathway for neuronal injury." Implicated in Alzheimer's, Huntington's, stroke, and other neurodegenerative conditions.

Hodgkin & Huxley (1952): When dendrites receive sufficient excitatory input from presynaptic neurons to reach threshold, voltage-gated sodium channels open and positively charged sodium ions (Na+) rush into the neuron, producing depolarization. This rapid influx of Na+ reverses the resting membrane potential from approximately -70 mV toward positive values, initiating the action potential.
FDG-PET is the most useful neuroimaging technique for distinguishing between neurocognitive disorder due to Alzheimer's disease and other neurocognitive disorders.
Bohnen et al. (2012): "FDG-PET is the most useful functional neuroimaging technique for differentiating Alzheimer's disease from other neurodegenerative dementias."

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
verbatim: Boccia, Piccardi, & Guariglia (2016): A meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies examining brain activity in major depressive disorder before and after psychotherapy found that successful psychotherapy was associated with decreased activity in the left precentral gyrus and increased activity in the cingulate cortex. These changes suggest that psychotherapy normalizes aberrant neural activity patterns in depression.

Engel (2001): Mesial temporal lobe seizures "typically beginning with an aura — most commonly an epigastric rising sensation, deja vu, fear, or olfactory hallucination — followed by behavioral arrest, staring, and oroalimentary automatisms (lip smacking, chewing, swallowing)."

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Moore (1997): The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the anterior hypothalamus is the "master circadian pacemaker in mammals." Regulates the sleep-wake cycle by modulating melatonin secretion from the pineal gland.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Alexander & Crutcher (1990): Cortical motor areas send excitatory projections to the striatum (caudate nucleus and putamen), the primary input nuclei of the basal ganglia. This corticostriatal input initiates processing for voluntary movement sequences.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Pattinson (2008): Opioid-induced respiratory depression is primarily mediated by mu-opioid receptors in the brainstem respiratory centers, particularly within the medulla oblongata. Opioid binding at these sites suppresses the medullary respiratory rhythm generator, reducing respiratory rate and tidal volume and, at toxic doses, causing fatal respiratory arrest.
Porges (2007): The parasympathetic nervous system promotes "rest-and-digest" functions restoring homeostatic baseline: decreases heart rate, lowers blood pressure, stimulates digestion.

Stable (2014): Neuroimaging techniques are classified as structural or functional. Structural techniques — CT and conventional MRI — visualize brain anatomy. Functional techniques — fMRI, PET, and SPECT — measure brain activity by detecting changes in blood flow, metabolism, or neurotransmitter activity. CT is a structural (not functional) neuroimaging technique despite sometimes being confused with functional methods.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Bechara, Damasio, & Damasio (2000): "Damage to the ventromedial sector disrupts social behavior profoundly. Previously well-adapted individuals become unable to observe social conventions and unable to decide advantageously on matters pertaining to their own lives. Remarkably, the patient's intellectual abilities are generally well preserved."

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Siegel, Roeling, Gregg, & Kruk (1999): Medial hypothalamic stimulation elicits affective (defensive) aggression. Lateral hypothalamic stimulation produces predatory (stalking) aggression.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Lupien, McEwen, Gunnar, & Heim (2009): "chronic exposure to stress hormones" has an impact on brain structures involved in cognition, impairing hippocampal-dependent declarative memory.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Moruzzi & Magoun (1949): Electrical stimulation of the brainstem reticular formation produced widespread cortical activation, shifting the EEG from synchronized (sleep) to desynchronized (wakefulness) patterns. The ascending reticular activating system (ARAS) functions as a nonspecific arousal mechanism.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Schmahmann (2004): Cerebellar dysfunction produces "incoordination of balance, gait, extremity and eye movements, and dysarthria." Damage produces ataxia and motor incoordination.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Cannon (1927): An emotion-provoking stimulus activates the thalamus, which simultaneously sends signals to the cortex (subjective experience) and to the autonomic nervous system/skeletal muscles (physiological arousal). Experience and arousal are concurrent and independent.

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Goldenberg (2009): Apraxia is "a disorder of motor control which can be referred neither to 'elemental' motor deficits nor to general cognitive impairment." Three domains are susceptible: imitation of gestures, performance of meaningful gestures on command, and use of tools and objects. Most commonly associated with left parietal lobe lesions.

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Vallar & Ronchi (2009): Asomatognosia is the lack of recognition or awareness of part of one's own body, most commonly the left hemiplegic limb following right parietal lobe damage. Frequently co-occurs with anosognosia — a lack of awareness or denial of one's illness or disability — and is associated with lesions in the right inferior parietal lobule and temporoparietal junction.

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Geschwind (1975): Ideomotor apraxia is "an inability to carry out learned, purposeful movements on verbal command or imitation, despite having the physical ability and desire to perform them." Most commonly results from damage to the left (dominant) parietal lobe, particularly the supramarginal gyrus, which stores motor representations (praxicons) for learned skilled actions.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Saper & Lowell (2014): The hypothalamus "contains highly conserved neural circuitry that controls basic life functions: these include energy metabolism, from feeding through digestion, metabolic control, and energy expenditure; fluid and electrolyte balance; thermoregulation; wake-sleep cycles; and reproduction."

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Sherman & Guillery (2002): "Virtually all information that reaches the cerebral cortex must first pass through the thalamus." First-order relays carry information from the sensory periphery (visual, auditory, tactile) to the cortex, with olfaction as the exception.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Stuss & Knight (2002): Frontal lobe injury consequences include reduced spontaneity and initiative in social interaction, emotional lability, and impaired planning and sequencing of goal-directed motor behavior.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Gazzaniga (1967): In split-brain patients with a severed corpus callosum, when a compound word such as "COPYCAT" is presented so that "COPY" falls in the left visual field (right hemisphere) and "CAT" in the right visual field (left hemisphere), the patient will verbally report "CAT" (left hemisphere language production) but will point to "COPY" with the left hand (right hemisphere motor control). The two hemispheres process their respective visual fields independently and cannot share information across the severed callosum.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Rasmussen & Milner (1977): Left-hemisphere language dominance occurred in approximately 96% of right-handed individuals and approximately 70% of left-handed individuals.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Heilman, Watson, & Valenstein (1993): Contralateral neglect (hemispatial neglect) is a failure to attend to, respond to, or orient toward stimuli in the contralesional hemispace. It is most commonly and most severely produced by damage to the right parietal lobe, particularly the inferior parietal lobule and temporoparietal junction. Patients may bump into objects on the neglected side, fail to eat food on one side of the plate, or draw only one half of objects.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Graybiel (2000): The basal ganglia are involved in the regulation of voluntary movement and consist of the caudate nucleus, putamen, and globus pallidus. "Lesions of the basal ganglia lead to devastating motor disorders, including Parkinson's disease and Huntington's disease." In addition, abnormalities in these structures have been linked to Tourette's disorder, ADHD, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: McGaugh (2004): "the BLA modulates the consolidation of memory of many different kinds of information" via its projections to other brain regions.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Geschwind (1965): Conduction aphasia results from disconnection between Wernicke's area and Broca's area due to arcuate fasciculus damage. Hallmark is "a disproportionate impairment of repetition in the face of fluent, well-articulated speech and relatively preserved auditory comprehension."

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Geschwind (1965): Conduction aphasia results from damage to the arcuate fasciculus, disconnecting Wernicke's area from Broca's area. Cardinal features include relatively intact auditory comprehension, fluent but paraphasic speech, anomia (impaired naming), and disproportionately impaired repetition — the hallmark deficit.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Green & Swets (1966): In signal detection theory, d-prime (d') is a measure of sensitivity — the ability to discriminate signal from noise. It is calculated as the difference between the z-transformed hit rate and the z-transformed false alarm rate: d' = z(hit rate) - z(false alarm rate). A higher d' indicates greater discriminability between signal and noise.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Squire (1992): "the hippocampus (together with anatomically related structures)" is essential for the formation of long-term declarative (explicit) memory — memory for facts and events that is accessible to conscious recollection. Bilateral hippocampal damage produces severe anterograde amnesia.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Sperry (1968): "visual material projected to the right half of the field... could be described in speech and writing in an essentially normal manner. When the same visual material was projected into the left half of the field... the subject consistently denied having seen anything, or any flash of light." The left hand (right hemisphere) could retrieve the correct object by touch.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Kimura (1967): Dichotic listening — when different auditory stimuli are simultaneously presented to each ear, most individuals show a right-ear advantage for verbal material (left hemisphere language dominance) and a left-ear advantage for nonverbal stimuli (right hemisphere specialization).

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Reed, Caselli, & Farah (1996): Tactile agnosia (astereognosis) is "a modality-specific impairment in object recognition through touch, in which basic somatosensory functions such as tactile acuity, pressure sensitivity, and proprioception remain intact." Tactile agnosia is caused by lesions in the parietal lobe, resulting in an inability to recognize familiar objects by touch despite intact somatosensory functions.

Stable (2005): Non-contrast CT is "the imaging modality of choice for the initial evaluation of acute traumatic brain injury." Preferred for rapid acquisition time, wide availability, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity for acute hemorrhage, hematomas, and contusions.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Kluver & Bucy (1939): Bilateral temporal lobectomy syndrome characterized by: (1) psychic blindness/visual agnosia, (2) hyperorality, (3) emotional changes including loss of fear, (4) hypersexuality, and (5) hyperphagia. In humans, occurs following bilateral temporal lobe damage involving the amygdala.

Bowman (2006): Curare is a competitive antagonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. It binds to the receptor without activating it, blocking acetylcholine from binding and preventing muscle depolarization. This produces skeletal muscle paralysis, including paralysis of the respiratory muscles at sufficient doses.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Sandson & Albert (1984): "Stuck-in-set perseveration, the inappropriate maintenance of a current category or framework, involves an underlying process deficit in executive functioning and is related neuroanatomically to frontal lobe damage." Patients repeat a previously correct response even after task demands have changed.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Ross (1981): The right hemisphere organizes affective components of language (prosody). Right hemisphere damage produces "aprosodias" -- impairments in production and/or comprehension of melodic, tonal, and rhythmic aspects of speech conveying emotion. Right hemisphere damage is also associated with impairments in pragmatics -- the ability to use language appropriately in social contexts, including understanding humor, sarcasm, indirect requests, and conversational inference.

Okun (2012): Current FDA-approved indications for deep brain stimulation (DBS) include Parkinson's disease, essential tremor, and dystonia. Atypical parkinsonism (e.g., progressive supranuclear palsy, multiple system atrophy) is a contraindication for DBS due to poor treatment response and accelerated decline.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Damasio (1992): Broca's aphasia involves "a drastic loss of speech fluency." Speech is "effortful and often slow" and "labored and flat, and the melodic modulation that characterizes normal speech is lacking (this type of speech is known as nonfluent)." "The hallmark of true Broca's aphasia is agrammatism." Patients can still communicate verbally; words tend to be intelligible, but grammatical words are less well selected. Comprehension is relatively preserved.

chapter_title: Language, Lateralization, and the Split Brain
verbatim: Gazzaniga, Bogen, & Sperry (1967): Patients had undergone complete surgical section of the corpus callosum as treatment for medically intractable epilepsy.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Asberg, Traskman, & Thoren (1976): Depressed patients with low CSF concentrations of 5-HIAA (primary serotonin metabolite) were significantly more likely to have attempted suicide and at greater risk for future attempts. Low CSF 5-HIAA is a biological marker of suicidal risk.

Hodgkin & Huxley (1952): The all-or-none law states that once membrane depolarization reaches threshold, an action potential fires at full, fixed amplitude regardless of stimulus intensity. Increasing stimulus strength above threshold does not increase the magnitude of the individual action potential; rather, stronger stimuli increase the frequency of action potentials (rate coding).

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: The Vital Infrastructure: Endocrine System, HPA Axis, and Neuroimaging
verbatim: Thomson, Cook, Touquet, & Henry (2002): "parenteral (intravenous) thiamine should be administered immediately in any patient suspected of having Wernicke's encephalopathy."

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Heilman, Valenstein, & Watson (2000): "Neglect is a failure to report, respond, or orient to contralateral stimuli that is not caused by an elemental sensorimotor deficit." Contralateral neglect is most commonly caused by damage to the parietal lobe, particularly the right parietal lobe. Right hemisphere lesions produce the most severe and persistent forms of contralateral neglect, and a person with this condition is unaware of objects and areas on the side of the body opposite the lesion.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Miller & Cohen (2001): The prefrontal cortex plays "an important role in cognitive control, in the ability to orchestrate thought and action in accordance with internal goals." Specifically, lesions in the dorsolateral area of the prefrontal cortex are most likely to cause deficits in executive functions including insight, planning, judgment, abstract reasoning, set-shifting, and behavioral monitoring. The dorsolateral PFC provides bias signals that guide the flow of activity along neural pathways needed for goal-directed behavior.
McClelland & Rumelhart (1981): Top-down influence explains how readers automatically correct typographical errors using stored word forms and sentence context.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Mohler (2006): GABA agonists "enhance the inhibitory action of GABA at GABA-A receptors, thereby reducing neuronal excitability."

Katz & Miledi (1967): Acetylcholine (ACh) is released from motor neuron terminals at the neuromuscular junction, where it binds to nicotinic receptors on the muscle fiber membrane, triggering depolarization and muscle contraction. ACh is the essential neurotransmitter for voluntary (skeletal) muscle movement.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Farah (2004): Prosopagnosia — "patients can perceive individual facial features and determine that a stimulus is a face (intact bottom-up processing) but cannot match the perceived face to stored identity representations (impaired top-down recognition)."

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Babinski (1914): Coined "anosognosia" to describe patients who were unaware of or denied their paralysis. Anosognosia is most commonly caused by damage to the right parietal lobe and is characterized by a lack of awareness of one's own physical disability. It is consistently associated with right hemisphere damage, particularly right parietal lesions.

Curcio, Sloan, Kalina, & Hendrickson (1990): "rod density peaks in a ring approximately 20 degrees from the fovea and remains high throughout the retinal periphery." Rods disproportionately mediate peripheral vision.

Hodgkin & Huxley (1952): "once membrane depolarization reaches threshold, the regenerative opening of voltage-gated sodium channels produces an action potential of fixed amplitude and duration." The all-or-none law: fires at full magnitude or not at all.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Wise (2004): "Dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens has been linked to the efficacy of these unconditioned rewards." Cocaine blocks the dopamine transporter; amphetamines block reuptake and stimulate reverse transport.

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Gerstmann (1940): A tetrad of symptoms from lesions of the dominant (typically left) parietal lobe: (1) finger agnosia, (2) right-left disorientation, (3) agraphia, and (4) acalculia. Ataxia is not part of this syndrome; ataxia results from cerebellar pathology.

Cryer (2007): Hypoglycemia produces: (1) autonomic symptoms — tremor, diaphoresis, hunger, pallor — and (2) neuroglycopenic symptoms — confusion, disorientation, irritability, dizziness, weakness, somnolence.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Hering (1878/1964): Color vision is mediated by three opponent neural channels: red vs. green, blue vs. yellow, black vs. white. Stimulation of one member inhibits the other. Explains negative afterimages: prolonged viewing of one color fatigues that channel, and the opponent process temporarily dominates.

Palmer (1999): Convergence is "the inward rotation of the eyes that occurs when fixating on a near object, and the degree of convergence provides a binocular (oculomotor) cue to the distance of the fixated object."

Cytowic (2002): Synesthesia is "an involuntary, automatic perceptual phenomenon in which stimulation of one sensory or cognitive pathway leads to an additional, concurrent experience in a second sensory or cognitive pathway."

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Wernicke (1874): Aphasia with severely impaired auditory and reading comprehension, fluent speech with paraphasias, neologisms, and semantic errors ("word salad"), and impaired repetition.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Tolin, Abramowitz, Brigidi, & Foa (2003): Intolerance of uncertainty — the tendency to react negatively to uncertain situations — has been associated with increased volume and altered activity in the striatum (caudate nucleus and putamen), a component of the basal ganglia. High scores on the Intolerance of Uncertainty Scale are linked to structural changes in the striatum, consistent with the striatum's role in habit formation and predictive processing.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Howard & Rogers (2012): Retinal (binocular) disparity is "the difference in the positions of corresponding points in the two retinal images that arises because the two eyes view the world from slightly different horizontal positions." Most effective at close distances; beyond that, monocular cues become primary.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Kandel (1976): The marine sea slug Aplysia californica was selected as a model organism for studying the neuronal basis of learning and memory because it possesses a simple nervous system of approximately 20,000 large, identifiable neurons. This simplicity allowed the mapping of specific synaptic changes — including enhanced neurotransmitter release and structural growth of new synaptic connections — underlying habituation, sensitization, and classical conditioning.
Weber (1834): The just noticeable difference (JND) is a constant proportion of the initial stimulus intensity. The JND increases proportionally with stimulus magnitude.

Stevens (1957): Stevens's power law states that the perceived magnitude of a stimulus is a power function of the physical stimulus intensity: P = kS^n, where P is perceived magnitude, S is stimulus intensity, k is a constant, and n is the exponent specific to the sensory modality. Derived using the method of magnitude estimation, in which participants assign numbers proportional to perceived intensity.

chapter_title: Parietal and Temporal Lobes: Sensation, Language Areas, and the Agnosias
verbatim: Bodamer (1947): "prosopagnosia" — patients lost the ability to recognize familiar faces, including their own reflection, while retaining the ability to recognize individuals by voice, gait, or other non-facial cues. The deficit is selective to face identity recognition.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Bliss & Lomo (1973): Long-term potentiation (LTP) — brief, high-frequency stimulation of hippocampal pathways produced sustained increase in synaptic transmission efficiency. Considered a primary neural mechanism underlying memory storage.

Walls (1942): "the ratio of rods to cones in the retina reflects an organism's adaptation to its photic environment." Rod-dominant retinae provide high scotopic sensitivity and broad peripheral vision. Cone-dominant retinae provide superior color vision and acuity.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Leiguarda & Marsden (2000): Limb-kinetic apraxia is "a loss of the ability to make finely graded, precise, individual finger and hand movements" not attributable to weakness, sensory loss, or incoordination. Tasks such as picking up a coin, buttoning, and rotating objects between fingers are characteristically impaired.

Gregory (1970): Perception is a constructive, hypothesis-driven process in which "the brain uses stored knowledge, expectations, and contextual information to generate perceptual hypotheses about the identity and meaning of sensory input." Top-down processing explains why perception is influenced by experience, motivation, and context.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Hobson, Pace-Schott, & Stickgold (2000): "dreaming is not confined to REM sleep; mentation reports can be obtained from NREM sleep awakenings." REM dreams are "longer, more vivid, more hallucinatory, more emotionally intense, more bizarre, and more readily recalled than NREM dream reports."

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Roffwarg, Muzio, & Dement (1966): Neonates begin each sleep period with active sleep, the developmental precursor of REM sleep. Active sleep in newborns is characterized by irregular respirations, rapid eye movements, facial grimaces, and limb twitches. This is the functional equivalent of stage R (REM) sleep in adults, which adults typically enter only after progressing through NREM stages.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: James (1884): "the bodily changes follow directly the perception of the exciting fact, and that our feeling of the same changes as they occur IS the emotion." The emotion does not cause the physiological response; rather, the physiological response produces the emotional experience ("we feel sad because we cry").

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Berry et al. (2017): NREM sleep has three stages: N1, N2, and N3. All NREM stages show progressively increasing amplitude and decreasing frequency reflecting increasing cortical synchronization.

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Gainotti (1972): Left brain-damaged patients show a significantly higher incidence of catastrophic reactions (anxiety, tears, aggressive behavior, swearing, depressive symptoms). Right hemisphere-damaged patients show significantly more indifference reactions (anosognosia, minimization of deficits, joking in a "fatuous, euphoric or ironical way").

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Strack, Martin, & Stepper (1988): "We investigated the hypothesis that people's facial activity influences their affective responses." Subjects reported more intense humor responses to cartoons under facilitating conditions (pen in teeth, activating smile muscles) than under inhibiting conditions (pen in lips). Consistent with James-Lange peripheral-first model.

Burgess, Quayle, & Frith (2001): The prefrontal cortex supports maintaining intentions, monitoring for appropriate cues, and switching attention to execute intended actions — critical for prospective memory processes.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Davis & Squire (1984): "inhibitors of protein synthesis, when administered around the time of training, prevent the formation of long-term memory while leaving short-term memory intact." Consolidation of short-term into long-term memory requires de novo protein synthesis.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Kandel (2001): In Aplysia, short-term memory involves covalent modification of pre-existing proteins and enhanced neurotransmitter release. Long-term memory requires new protein synthesis and growth of new synaptic connections. Learning produces structural and functional changes at the synapse.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Duffy & Czeisler (2002): Healthy older adults show an advance in circadian phase relative to younger adults, with earlier sleep-wake timing and a shift toward greater morningness preference. A shortening of circadian period with age cannot account for the advanced circadian phase.

chapter_title: Sleep Across the Lifespan and Sensation/Perception
verbatim: Roffwarg, Muzio, & Dement (1966): "the neonate enters sleep through an initial period of active (REM) sleep, in contrast to the NREM-onset pattern that characterizes sleep in older children and adults." This pattern persists through approximately the first three months of life.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Kryger, Roth, & Dement (2017): Delta waves are "high-amplitude (>75 microvolts), low-frequency (0.5-4 Hz) EEG oscillations that define slow-wave sleep (stage N3)."

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Schachter & Singer (1962): "An emotional state may be considered a function of a state of physiological arousal and of a cognition appropriate to this state of arousal." An important assumption of their two-factor theory is that the bodily responses (physiological arousal) associated with different emotions are essentially the same -- what differs is the cognitive interpretation of that arousal. Participants injected with epinephrine adopted the emotional tone of a confederate, labeling identical arousal as euphoria or anger depending on context.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Sterling & Eyer (1988): Allostasis refers to the process of achieving stability through physiological change. Unlike homeostasis, which implies a fixed set point, allostasis emphasizes that the body actively adjusts cardiovascular, neuroendocrine, and immune parameters to meet anticipated demands. McEwen (1998) extended this concept with "allostatic load" — the cumulative wear and tear resulting from chronic stress or inefficient allostatic responses.

chapter_title: The Biology of Memory and Sleep
verbatim: Ribot (1882): Ribot's law of regression states that in retrograde amnesia, more recently formed memories are more vulnerable to disruption than older, well-consolidated memories. During recovery, many patients exhibit "shrinking retrograde amnesia," in which the temporal extent of the amnesia contracts progressively — with the most distant (remote) memories returning first and the most recent pre-injury memories returning last or remaining permanently lost.

Stable (2005): Computed tomography (CT) is generally considered the most useful neuroimaging technique for the initial assessment of acute traumatic brain injury. CT is preferred because of its rapid acquisition time (seconds vs. minutes for MRI), wide availability in emergency departments, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity for acute hemorrhage, hematomas, skull fractures, and contusions. MRI requires the patient to remain motionless for an extended period, which is often impractical in acute trauma.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Lazarus (1991/1993): According to Lazarus's cognitive appraisal theory, primary appraisal involves evaluating whether an external event is (1) irrelevant, (2) benign-positive, or (3) stressful. When appraised as stressful, the event is further classified as involving harm (damage already done), threat (anticipated harm), or challenge (difficult demands one feels confident about overcoming). "Appraisal is a universal process in which people constantly evaluate the significance of what is happening for their personal well-being."

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Vonsattel & DiFiglia (1998): Huntington's disease neuropathology is "characterized by progressive degeneration of the striatum (caudate nucleus and putamen), with selective loss of GABAergic neurons." Disrupts basal ganglia pathways. Glutamate excitotoxicity contributes to selective neuronal vulnerability.

Caplan (2009): A stroke involving the middle cerebral artery (MCA) affecting the dominant hemisphere is most likely to produce contralateral hemiparesis (face and arm greater than leg), contralateral homonymous hemianopia, dysarthria, and aphasia (Broca's, Wernicke's, or global, depending on the extent and location of the infarct). The MCA supplies the lateral cerebral cortex, including language areas in the dominant hemisphere.

Aylward et al. (2004): "striatal (caudate and putamen) atrophy begins long before the onset of clinically diagnosable motor symptoms in Huntington's disease gene carriers." PET shows reduced glucose metabolism in caudate and putamen during the presymptomatic phase.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
verbatim: Papez (1937): A circuit of hippocampus, cingulate gyrus, mammillary bodies, and anterior thalamic nuclei as the neural substrate of emotional experience, emotional expression, and encoding of emotionally significant memories.

chapter_title: Emotion, Arousal, and Stress: The Feeling Brain
