verbatim: Gainotti (1972): Patients with damage to the right (nondominant) hemisphere frequently exhibit an "indifference reaction" — characterized by anosognosia, emotional blunting, inappropriate unconcern, or euphoria. Patients with left (dominant) hemisphere damage are more likely to display a "catastrophic reaction" — marked by anxiety, tearfulness, depression, and emotional volatility.
Oparil et al. (2018): Primary (essential) hypertension "has no single identifiable etiology." Secondary hypertension "results from an identifiable underlying medical condition."

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Kalia & Lang (2015): Parkinson's disease is a neurological disorder characterized by the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex II: Basal Ganglia, Brainstem, and Cerebellum
verbatim: Manto et al. (2012): The cerebellum plays a key role in motor control. Ataxia — impaired coordination, postural instability, and gait disturbance — is not exclusively cerebellar: sensory ataxia results from proprioceptive impairment, and vestibular ataxia from vestibular system dysfunction.

Garber et al. (2012): Hypothyroidism reflects "decreased metabolic rate due to insufficient circulating thyroid hormone." Symptoms include fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, and cognitive slowing.

Fisher et al. (2017): Focal aware seizures (formerly simple partial) originate in one hemisphere where "awareness is retained throughout the seizure." May manifest with motor symptoms (clonic jerking, eye blinking) or sensory symptoms.

Ribot (1882): "Shrinking retrograde amnesia" refers to the gradual recovery pattern following a head injury in which the temporal extent of retrograde amnesia progressively contracts. Older, well-consolidated long-term memories return before more recent pre-injury memories, consistent with Ribot's law that recently formed memories are more vulnerable to disruption than remote memories.

ICHD-3 (2018): Migraine with aura involves unilateral, fully reversible visual, sensory, or other central nervous system symptoms usually followed by headache. Headache is unilateral, pulsating, aggravated by physical activity, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and photophobia and/or phonophobia.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Neubig, Spedding, Kenakin, & Christopoulos (2003): An agonist is "a ligand that binds to a receptor and alters the receptor state resulting in a biological response." An antagonist produces no intrinsic effect on its own but "reduces the action of another drug, generally an agonist" by blocking the action of a neurotransmitter at receptor sites or by inhibiting its synthesis or release.

De Leo, Lee, & Braverman (2016): Hyperthyroidism is "a condition resulting from excess thyroid hormone production, leading to a hypermetabolic state." Features include tachycardia, tremor, diaphoresis, heat intolerance, unintentional weight loss, insomnia, and anxiety.

Scoville & Milner (1957): Patient H.M. underwent bilateral medial temporal lobe resection and exhibited "a grave loss of recent memory" — profound anterograde amnesia for declarative information with preserved short-term/working memory and preserved procedural learning.

Di Iorgi et al. (2012): Central diabetes insipidus is "a condition resulting from deficient secretion of antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) by the posterior pituitary gland." Results in excretion of large volumes of dilute urine and compensatory polydipsia.

Sacco et al. (2013): Ischemic stroke is defined as "central nervous system infarction accompanied by overt symptoms," caused by ischemia. Hemorrhagic stroke results from rupture of a cerebral blood vessel.

Caplan (2009): "bilateral vision loss results from bilateral posterior cerebral artery occlusion affecting the primary visual cortex in both occipital lobes." The MCA does not supply the occipital cortex; bilateral vision loss is not an MCA stroke presentation.

Ohayon, Carskadon, Guilleminault, & Vitiello (2004): Aging is associated with increased wake after sleep onset, increased sleep onset latency, decreased sleep efficiency, and reduced slow-wave (N3) sleep. Total sleep need does not significantly decline with age; the ability to sustain consolidated nocturnal sleep deteriorates.

chapter_title: The Frontal Lobe: Executive Function, Motor Control, and Prefrontal Syndromes
verbatim: Twitchell (1951): Hemiplegia is the loss of voluntary movement on one side of the body caused by thrombosis or embolism of cerebral blood vessels. Because corticospinal tracts decussate, a lesion in one hemisphere produces motor deficits on the contralateral side.

Ribot (1882): Ribot's law — "In progressive amnesia, the destruction advances progressively from the unstable to the stable, from the recent to the remote." In recovery, most remote memories return first; most recent pre-injury memories return last or may be permanently lost (shrinking retrograde amnesia).
Service (1995): Endogenous hyperinsulinism results in inappropriately elevated insulin levels that drive excessive glucose uptake, producing hypoglycemia.

Okun (2012): Deep brain stimulation is "a neurosurgical procedure in which electrodes are implanted in specific brain targets to deliver continuous electrical stimulation that modulates pathological neural activity." Indicated for Parkinson's disease, essential tremor, and dystonia. Atypical parkinsonism is a contraindication.

Fisher et al. (2017): Generalized onset seizures "originating at some point within, and rapidly engaging, bilaterally distributed networks." Due to bilateral cortical involvement, they invariably impair consciousness.

chapter_title: The Language of the Brain: Neurons, Neurotransmitters, and Neural Signaling
verbatim: Dale (1914): Acetylcholine (ACh) acts on two major classes of receptors: nicotinic receptors (ionotropic, found at neuromuscular junctions, autonomic ganglia, and in the CNS) and muscarinic receptors (metabotropic, found in the heart, smooth muscle, glands, and CNS). ACh is the primary neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction, in preganglionic autonomic neurons, and in parasympathetic postganglionic neurons.
De Gennaro & Ferrara (2003): Stage N2 features theta-frequency background with sleep spindles — "brief bursts of 9-16 Hz oscillations" — and K-complexes — "large-amplitude, biphasic waveforms."

Francis, Palmer, Snape, & Wilcock (1999): The cholinergic hypothesis: loss of acetylcholine in the cerebral cortex and hippocampus contributes significantly to the cognitive decline observed in Alzheimer's disease. Accompanied by elevated glutamate activity causing excitotoxic damage.

subdomain_name: Brain Regions/Functions - Hindbrain, Midbrain, and Subcortical Forebrain Structures
chapter_title: Below the Cortex I: Thalamus, Hypothalamus, and the Limbic System
verbatim: Gimpl & Fahrenholz (2001): Oxytocin is synthesized in the hypothalamus and released into the systemic circulation from the posterior pituitary. Primary functions include uterine contraction during labor and milk ejection (let-down reflex) during breastfeeding.

Basser, Mattiello, & LeBihan (1994): Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) is a specialized MRI technique that measures the diffusion of water molecules along white matter tracts. Because water diffuses preferentially along the direction of myelinated axon bundles (anisotropic diffusion), DTI can map the structural connectivity and integrity of white matter pathways in the brain. Used to detect abnormalities in white matter associated with traumatic brain injury, multiple sclerosis, and neurodegenerative disorders.

Canevelli et al. (2016): Sundowning is "a chronobiological phenomenon characterized by the emergence or exacerbation of neuropsychiatric symptoms (agitation, confusion, wandering, aggression, disorientation) in the late afternoon and evening hours." Characteristic of neurocognitive disorders. In pseudodementia, cognitive symptoms are more constant, making evening exacerbation a discriminator.

Johnson (2005): Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) typically has an insidious onset with initial symptoms including cognitive decline, behavioral changes, and visual disturbances, followed by a characteristically rapid deterioration. Most patients with sporadic CJD progress from initial symptoms to death within 12 months, making it one of the most rapidly progressive neurocognitive disorders.

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2013): The diagnosis of probable major or mild neurocognitive disorder with Lewy bodies requires at least two of the following core features, or one core feature plus one or more suggestive features. Core features: (1) fluctuating cognition with pronounced variations in attention and alertness, (2) recurrent visual hallucinations that are typically well formed and detailed, (3) spontaneous features of parkinsonism with onset subsequent to the development of cognitive decline. Suggestive features include REM sleep behavior disorder, severe neuroleptic sensitivity, and low dopamine transporter uptake in the basal ganglia.

Corder et al. (1993): "the APOE-e4 allele is a major risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease, with risk increasing and age of onset decreasing in a dose-dependent manner." The most replicated genetic risk factor, though neither necessary nor sufficient for disease.

Braak & Del Tredici (2011): The locus coeruleus, a noradrenergic nucleus in the brainstem, has been identified as one of the earliest sites of tau pathology in Alzheimer's disease — sometimes referred to as "ground zero." Tau-containing pretangle neurons appear in the locus coeruleus decades before the onset of clinical symptoms, prior to involvement of the entorhinal cortex and hippocampus.

McKhann et al. (2011): The most common Alzheimer's presentation involves impairment in the ability to learn and retain new information (anterograde episodic memory deficit), meeting criteria for probable AD dementia.

McKeith et al. (2017): The "one-year rule": "In DLB, dementia occurs before or concurrently with parkinsonism. In Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD), dementia develops in the context of well-established Parkinson's disease."

Rascovsky et al. (2011): bvFTD requires at least three of six features: "(1) early behavioral disinhibition, (2) early apathy or inertia, (3) early loss of sympathy or empathy, (4) early perseverative, stereotyped, or compulsive/ritualistic behavior, (5) hyperorality and dietary changes, and (6) neuropsychological profile showing executive/generation deficits with relative sparing of memory and visuospatial functions."

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Terracciano et al. (2014): "high neuroticism and low conscientiousness were significantly and independently associated with increased risk of developing Alzheimer's disease."

Devanand et al. (2015): "olfactory identification deficits predicted the transition from normal cognition to mild cognitive impairment and from mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer's disease." Rapid olfactory decline is an early clinical indicator.

Wiseman et al. (2015): Individuals with standard trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) have three copies of chromosome 21, which contains the amyloid precursor protein (APP) gene. Overexpression of APP due to the extra gene copy leads to increased production and accumulation of amyloid-beta peptide, resulting in the near-universal development of Alzheimer's-type neuropathology by age 40 and significantly elevated risk for early-onset Alzheimer's dementia.

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: McKeith et al. (2017): DLB motor symptoms (rigidity, bradykinesia, gait disturbance) appear early and cause greater functional disability than early-stage Alzheimer's, where cognitive deficits predominate.

O'Brien & Thomas (2015): Vascular neurocognitive disorder course is "variable, reflecting the heterogeneity of the underlying cerebrovascular disease." May include abrupt onset and stepwise deterioration, or gradual progressive decline. May include plateaus, fluctuations, and relative stability.

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: McKeith et al. (2017): The core clinical features of neurocognitive disorder with Lewy bodies include fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, and parkinsonism. Perseverative or compulsive behavior is not a characteristic feature of Lewy body dementia; rather, it is a diagnostic criterion for behavioral variant frontotemporal neurocognitive disorder (Rascovsky et al., 2011).

Braak & Braak (1991): In earliest Alzheimer's stages (Braak I-II), "neurofibrillary pathology is largely confined to the transentorhinal region." Pathology then spreads to the entorhinal cortex and hippocampus in later stages.

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Francis, Palmer, Snape, & Wilcock (1999): "the cholinergic hypothesis proposes that the deficit of acetylcholine in the hippocampus and neocortex is a major contributor to the cognitive and memory dysfunction characteristic of Alzheimer's disease."

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Braak & Braak (1991): The neuropathology of Alzheimer's disease involves progressive accumulation of neuritic (amyloid) plaques and neurofibrillary tangles. The earliest changes (Braak stages I-II) appear in the transentorhinal and entorhinal cortex, then spread to the hippocampus (stages III-IV) and amygdala, and eventually to widespread neocortical areas (stages V-VI).

chapter_title: When the Brain Breaks: Neurocognitive and Neurological Disorders
verbatim: Johnson (2005): Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is a degenerative disorder of the nervous system caused by misfolded prion proteins. Its exceptionally rapid course distinguishes CJD from all other neurocognitive disorders.

Cummings (1986): "Subcortical dementia refers to a clinical syndrome characterised by slowing of cognition, memory disturbances, difficulty with complex intellectual tasks such as strategy generation and problem solving, visuospatial abnormalities, and disturbances of mood and affect." Language is largely spared in subcortical dementias, unlike cortical dementias such as Alzheimer's which produce aphasia.

Johnson (2005): Neurocognitive disorder caused by prion disease (e.g., Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease) is not potentially reversible. Prion diseases involve the progressive accumulation of misfolded prion proteins (PrPSc) that cause irreversible spongiform degeneration of brain tissue. No effective treatment exists, and the disease is invariably fatal, typically within one year of symptom onset.

chapter_title: Beyond the Clinician's Hands: Automated Scoring, Delegation, and the Boundaries of Test Use
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.09: Psychologists may utilize automated test scoring and interpretation services but remain responsible for the appropriate application, interpretation, and use of the results.

chapter_title: The Therapeutic Perimeter: Standard 10 and the Ethics of Clinical Relationships
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 3.05: A psychologist may maintain a social relationship with the relative of a former client provided it does not compromise professional objectivity or risk exploitation.

chapter_title: The Therapeutic Perimeter: Standard 10 and the Ethics of Clinical Relationships
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.10(b): A psychologist may terminate therapy when threatened or otherwise endangered by the client or another person with whom the client has a relationship, and is not required to provide pretermination counseling under such circumstances.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: APA (2020): APA Publication Manual guidelines require bias-free language that puts the person first (e.g., "adults with a mental illness").

chapter_title: Guarding the Gate: Ethical Foundations of Assessment Under Standard 9
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.06: When evaluating examinees with limited English proficiency, a psychologist may use a qualified interpreter and must document possible limitations of the results.

chapter_title: Building Competence, Protecting the Public: Clinical Supervision and Early Intelligence Patterns
verbatim: Falender & Shafranske (2004): When a supervisee's impaired performance is circumscribed to a particular rotation and attributable to an identifiable time-limited stressor, the appropriate supervisory response is to transfer the supervisee to a different rotation.

chapter_title: Building Competence, Protecting the Public: Clinical Supervision and Early Intelligence Patterns
verbatim: Stoltenberg & McNeill (2010): The Integrated Developmental Model (IDM) of supervision characterizes supervisee development along three dimensions: self-other awareness, motivation, and autonomy.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: ASPPB (2015): The primary supervisor of a psychology intern must hold a current license in psychology.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: ASPPB (2017): The ASPPB E.Passport is an interjurisdictional practice certificate that facilitates the provision of telepsychology services across state or provincial boundaries.

chapter_title: Psychology in the Courtroom: Forensic Evaluation, Expert Testimony, and Legal Procedure
verbatim: Melton et al. (2018); Dusky v. United States (1960): Competency to stand trial evaluations assess the defendant's capacity to understand the charges, appreciate potential penalties, and cooperate meaningfully with defense counsel; a finding of incompetency suspends proceedings until competency is restored through treatment.

chapter_title: Building Competence, Protecting the Public: Clinical Supervision and Early Intelligence Patterns
verbatim: APA (2014): A supervisor's primary ethical and legal obligation is safeguarding the welfare of the supervisee's clients.

chapter_title: The Therapeutic Perimeter: Standard 10 and the Ethics of Clinical Relationships
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.10: When a psychologist's preferred treatment modality requires participation from family members who are unwilling to attend, the psychologist should refer the client to a provider whose approach can accommodate the available participants.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: Steadman et al. (1993): The insanity defense is raised in fewer than 1% of all felony cases and results in acquittal approximately 25% of the time.

chapter_title: Closing the Loop: Termination Ethics, Crisis Boundaries, and the Bridge to Assessment
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 2.01: When providing crisis intervention and long-term therapy is outside one's competence area, the appropriate course of action is to provide short-term crisis services and then refer the client for ongoing treatment.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: Corey, Corey, & Callanan (2019): Vicarious liability is the legal doctrine under which an employer is held responsible for the professional acts of employees performed within the scope of employment.

chapter_title: The Therapeutic Perimeter: Standard 10 and the Ethics of Clinical Relationships
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.03: In group therapy, psychologists are obligated to discuss the importance of maintaining confidentiality with group members during the initial session and at appropriate points thereafter.

chapter_title: Beyond the Clinician's Hands: Automated Scoring, Delegation, and the Boundaries of Test Use
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.08: Psychologists may use obsolete tests or outdated test data under certain limited circumstances as specified by ethical guidelines.

chapter_title: Psychology in the Courtroom: Forensic Evaluation, Expert Testimony, and Legal Procedure
verbatim: Shneidman (1981): A psychological autopsy is conducted to clarify the manner of death when circumstances are equivocal.

chapter_title: Psychology in the Courtroom: Forensic Evaluation, Expert Testimony, and Legal Procedure
verbatim: APA Commission on Accreditation (2015): APA accreditation of graduate programs serves the dual purpose of protecting public welfare and safeguarding the interests of students.

chapter_title: Guarding the Gate: Ethical Foundations of Assessment Under Standard 9
verbatim: AERA, APA, & NCME (2014): Test modifications alter the construct being measured by the original test, whereas test accommodations change administration conditions without affecting the measured construct.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries
verbatim: PSYPACT (2019): A psychologist may continue videoconferencing therapy with a client who relocates to another state only if both states participate in PSYPACT or the destination state's laws permit out-of-state practice.

chapter_title: Guarding the Gate: Ethical Foundations of Assessment Under Standard 9
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.03: Psychologists may use assessment results for both clinical and research purposes provided informed consent is obtained for each distinct use.

chapter_title: Guarding the Gate: Ethical Foundations of Assessment Under Standard 9
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.02(b): Psychologists must use assessment instruments whose validity and reliability have been established for the purpose for which they are being used; using a personality test that has not been validated as a predictor of job performance for a specific occupation would violate this standard.

chapter_title: Guarding the Gate: Ethical Foundations of Assessment Under Standard 9
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.11: Providing a client's attorney with actual test items and item-level responses may violate ethical obligations to maintain test security, even when the client authorizes release.

chapter_title: Beyond the Clinician's Hands: Automated Scoring, Delegation, and the Boundaries of Test Use
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 9.08: Psychologists must decline requests to base professional decisions primarily on obsolete or outdated test results and should recommend current, validated assessment procedures.

chapter_title: Beyond the Clinician's Hands: Automated Scoring, Delegation, and the Boundaries of Test Use
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 4.01: When a supervisor holds legal responsibility for a psychology intern's actions, the intern must disclose the supervisory arrangement to clients and provide the supervisor's name.

chapter_title: Closing the Loop: Termination Ethics, Crisis Boundaries, and the Bridge to Assessment
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.10: When a client or third party associated with the client threatens the therapist with physical harm, the therapist may terminate treatment with or without providing pretermination counseling.

chapter_title: Closing the Loop: Termination Ethics, Crisis Boundaries, and the Bridge to Assessment
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.07: Psychologists are prohibited from providing therapy to individuals with whom they previously had a sexual relationship; this prohibition applies regardless of time elapsed.

chapter_title: The Therapeutic Perimeter: Standard 10 and the Ethics of Clinical Relationships
verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.04: When a client discloses concurrent treatment with another mental health professional, the psychologist should discuss the situation with the client to understand the rationale and determine the most appropriate course of action.

chapter_title: Building Competence, Protecting the Public: Clinical Supervision and Early Intelligence Patterns

chapter_title: Psychology in the Courtroom: Forensic Evaluation, Expert Testimony, and Legal Procedure

verbatim: APA (2017), Standard 10.04: When a psychologist learns that a client is also receiving mental health services from another professional, the psychologist should discuss the situation with the client to minimize confusion and potential conflict, and determine the most appropriate course of action.

chapter_title: The Legal Landscape: Licensure, Malpractice, and Professional Boundaries

chapter_title: The Wechsler Architecture: Scales, Scores, and Clinical Profiles

verbatim: MacLeod (1991): The Stroop Color-Word Association Test is fundamentally a measure of response inhibition.

verbatim: Hagen, Malkmus, & Durham (1972; revised 2000): Level V (Confused/Inappropriate, Non-Agitated) on the Rancho Los Amigos Scale of Cognitive Functioning-Revised describes a patient who is confused but not agitated, responds to simple commands, lacks orientation to person, place, or time, has difficulty learning new information, and may wander with a vague intention of going home.

In psychological assessment, 'floor' and 'ceiling' describe how well an instrument differentiates among examinees at the extreme ends of the trait being measured. verbatim: Wechsler (2008): The WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index comprises the Symbol Search and Coding subtests, with Cancellation as a supplemental subtest.

verbatim: Roid (2003): The SB5 is grounded in the Cattell-Horn-Carroll (CHC) model and yields scores on five factors: knowledge, fluid reasoning, quantitative reasoning, visual-spatial processing, and working memory.

verbatim: Wechsler (2008): Individuals with ADHD typically obtain their highest WAIS-IV score on the Verbal Comprehension Index relative to other index scores.

In legal terminology, a tort refers to a civil wrong stemming from a negligent breach of duty that causes harm or injury to a patient, and torts fall into two categories: intentional and unintentional. Intentional torts include conversion (theft or damage to personal property), infliction of emotional distress, and fraud/deceit, whereas unintentional torts—also labeled negligence torts—encompass acts like malpractice. A malpractice claim requires four elements: (a) a professional relationship existed between psychologist and patient, creating a duty to meet a professional standard of care; (b) the psychologist breached that duty through negligence; (c) the patient sustained harm or injury; and (d) the breach was the direct or proximate cause of that harm.
